action_tool_name: Standardizing Permitting for Emergency, Affordable and Middle Housing
reform_category: Promote Certainty in Permitting Process,Shift Culture
description: The Washington 2025 legislative session took action to standardize the permitting process for emergency, affordable and middle housing in counties and cities planning under the Growth Management Act (GMA). One law reformed building codes for GMA-planning counties to ease affordable housing permitting and required middle housing sizes be comparable with average the market-rate size. A second law barred cities and counties out of compliance with GMA housing requirements from denying or imposing overly burdensome conditions on affordable housing. A third law required middle housing in GMA-planning counties to face permitting and environmental review no stricter than single-family homes in urban growth areas and similar designated zones. The overall impact is to standardize and streamline permitting for affordable and middle housing by removing local barriers and tying permitting authority to state growth-management.

action_tool_name: Document Search Portal
reform_category: Utilize Data and Technology
description: The DEEP Document Online Search Portal includes a collection of documents electronically produced or digitally scanned by the Agency. The portal is searchable by a variety of fields.

action_tool_name: Capping Permitting and Building Fees
reform_category: Promote Certainty in Permitting Process
description: The Act caps and defines “reasonable” permitting fees by providing that county siting or special use application fees and building permit fees for commercial wind and solar are presumptively reasonable only up to specified dollar limits per megawatt of nameplate capacity (with total caps), thereby limiting the use of high local fees as an indirect barrier to obtaining permits.

action_tool_name: Approving Minor Modifications to an Existing Structure on a Resort
reform_category: Shift Culture,Consolidate or Eliminate Steps
description: Florida Chapter 2026-7 requires local governments to approve, without further action, any application for a modification, or refurbishment of an existing structure or site on the resort property that is not a contributing structure listed in the National Register of Historic Places.

action_tool_name: Enhancing environmental oversight for emerging infrastructure
reform_category: Shift Culture,Benefits for Nature/Communities
description: - Requiring the Commission to complete (i) an environmental impact statement (EIS) under the Minnesota Environmental Policy Act, Minn. Stat. Ch. 116D and Minn. R. 4410, for all carbon dioxide (CO2) pipelines, and (ii) a general study evaluating specific issues related to routing CO2 pipelines

action_tool_name: Protecting Minimum Square Footage Floors and Siting Standards for Manufactured Homes
reform_category: Consolidate or Eliminate Steps,Change Decision-Making Level,Promote Certainty in Permitting Process
description:
A 2026 Executive Order requires that manufacture homes being allowed on any lot zoned for single family housing. State law now supersedes local zoning authority, hence setting equal requirements for manufactured home siting. 
Local governments cannot require manufactured homes to be larger than the minimums of 400 square feet (single-section homes), or 800 square feet (multi-section homes) as a condition of siting approval.

action_tool_name: Creating Advisory Bodies to Speed Development Projects
reform_category: Change Decision-Making Level
action_tool_type: Reorganization or Creation of New Government Offices
description:
Housing Advisory Council - EO 5-2022 created the Council and EO 3-2023 continued it past its initial term. The Council during its terms recommended and helped to implement several permitting-related reforms, such as eliminating certain public hearings and establishing standard timelines for permit approvals.

Energy Task Force - EO 6-2025 created the task force with the specific strategy of working towards more efficient permitting for energy projects

Red Tape Relief Advisory Council - EO 1-2021 created the Council, with members across 13 state agencies, to identify and eliminate burdensome regulations, including those regarding permitting.

action_tool_name: Requiring Oregon Department of Energy and Public Utilities Commission with developing a transmission corridor strategic framework for the Legislature.
reform_category: Change Decision-Making Level,Promote Certainty in Permitting Process,Utilize Data and Technology
description: EO 25-29 assigned Oregon Department of Energy (ODOE) and Public Utilities Commission (PUC) as co-leads on developing a strategic framework for transmission corridor designation, to be delivered to the Oregon Legislature as the basis for future action. Prior to this assignment, corridor decisions had been handled on a project-by-project basis through Energy Facility Siting Council's site certificate process. No agency had a standing charge to produce a statewide transmission planning document. In practice, ODOE and PUC are now expected to identify specific geographic corridors where transmission infrastructure should be prioritized (for example, connecting eastern Oregon wind and solar resources to the Willamette Valley), recommend which projects should receive targeted state financial support, and propose streamlining mechanisms such as pre-cleared land use determinations for corridor-aligned projects.

action_tool_name: Establishing the Housing Production Advisory Council and set a statewide housing production goal of 36,000 units per year
reform_category: Shift Culture,Set Projects up for Success,Building the Permitting Workforce,Promote Certainty in Permitting Process
action_tool_type: Other,Reorganization or Creation of New Government Offices,Regulatory Change
description: EO 23-04 established the Housing Production Advisory Council (HPAC) and set a statewide target of 36,000 housing units per year, an 80% increase over prevailing construction rates. The Council was tasked with producing an action plan addressing land use, permitting, and workforce barriers. Its February 2024 final report was substantially incorporated into SB 1537 (2024), which created the Housing Accountability and Production Office and established permitting streamlining and UGB expansion mechanisms.

action_tool_name: Electronic Natural Diversity Database (NDDB)
reform_category: Utilize Data and Technology
description:
This automated review system determines whether proposed projects impact endangered, threatened, or special concern species through real-time GIS intersection analysis. In its first five months, NDDB processed 447 applications with **42% receiving immediate automated approval**—eliminating 191 manual biologist reviews and freeing specialized staff for complex cases requiring expert judgment. Previously, 100% of applications required manual review with wait times up to two months. The system achieved **100% immediate determination** of review type required and exceeded its 20BY20 goal of 60% immediate response.

Zero downtime occurred in the first five months, and the system earned NASCIO (National Association of State Chief Information Officers) award recognition in 2023 for business process innovation. 

User testimonials emphasized simplicity: "WOW, I did not think this was going to be so simple" and "I hit submit and as soon as it finished processing the decision letter hit my email."

action_tool_name: Promoting Government Accountability in Permitting
reform_category: Shift Culture,Promote Certainty in Permitting Process
description: WA Chapter 235, 2026 Laws (Concerning Permit Review Processes) clarifies deadlines that local governments, special purpose districts and public utilities districts and other government agencies are subject to, with refunds as penalties if deadlines are not met. It also changes the requirements for approving permitting applications to be procedural rather than substantive.

action_tool_name: Permitting Decision Keys
reform_category: Promote Certainty in Permitting Process,Set Projects up for Success
description: These step-by-step guides help users understand how DEQ makes decisions related to permits, funding, and other approval processes. By walking through key questions, users can identify what types of approvals, registrations, or funding opportunities may apply to their activities or projects. While the keys cover most scenarios, some situations may require direct assistance or coordination with DEQ.

action_tool_name: Streamlining Wireless Telecommunications Permitting Processes
reform_category: Consolidate or Eliminate Steps,Promote Certainty in Permitting Process
description:
HB25-1056: 
- Sets deemed-approval timelines: 90 days for collocation/small cell applications; 150 days for all other wireless facility applications
- Application is deemed approved if local government fails to act within timeframe after required notice
- Requires local governments to identify application incompleteness within 30 days
- Allows tolling for information requests; mutual agreement extensions permitted
- Prohibits local governments from unreasonably withholding or delaying wireless permits
- Permits removal/replacement of equipment that is not a substantial change without new permits
- Allows up to 45-day tolling if reviewing affordable housing, renewable energy, or government projects simultaneously
EO_Leg_ID:
HB25-1056 - Local Government Permitting Wireless
Telecommunications Facilities

action_tool_name: Maryland OneStop Portal
reform_category: Set Projects up for Success,Promote Certainty in Permitting Process
description: The **Maryland OneStop portal** (onestop.md.gov) is the central digital hub for Maryland state government, designed to allow users to apply for, renew, and manage licenses, permits, registrations, and forms across various state agencies. It acts as a single point of entry intended to modernize, streamline, and centralize paper-based processes.

action_tool_name: Appointing Authorities for Approval of Plats
reform_category: Change Decision-Making Level
description:
Florida Chapter 2025-164 requires local governments to review, process, and approve plats or replat submittals through an administrative authority and official designated by ordinance without further action or approval by the governing body.
In Florida law, a “plat” is a representation of the subdivision of lands.

action_tool_name: Creating a Permitting Fast-track Program for Priority Housing Projects
Establishing the Office of Performance Management and Innovation
reform_category: Shift Culture
action_tool_type: Reorganization or Creation of New Government Offices
description: In 2026, Governor Hochul established a new office to optimize how state agencies work and collaborate, with a special focus on using technology to improve service delivery.

action_tool_name: Reviewing Permitting Processes to Identify Improvements
reform_category: Utilize Data and Technology,Promote Certainty in Permitting Process
EO_Leg_ID: EO 26-04 - EXECUTIVE ORDER TO MAKE ARKANSAS THE BEST STATE IN AMERICA TO DO BUSINESS THROUGH STREAMLINED, CLEAR, AND EXPEDITIOUS PERMITTING PROCESSES THAT PROTECT NATURAL RESOURCES AND FOSTER ECONOMIC DEVELOPMENT

action_tool_name: Eliminating State Ban on New Nuclear Energy
reform_category: Promote Certainty in Permitting Process
description: The Clean and Reliable Grid Affordability Act ended a 40-year prohibition on the construction of new, large-scale nuclear reactors over 300 megawatts.
EO_Leg_ID: SB0025, 104th General Assembly - Clean and Reliable Grid Affordability Act

action_tool_name: Providing State Resources to Facilitate Local Energy Project Siting
reform_category: Set Projects up for Success
description:
SB24-212 directs state agencies to produce and provide resources that facilitate local energy projects. Resources refer to technical support, template codes and ordinances, a comprehensive report on local permitting and siting processes, research on protected wildlife habitats and environments for developers to avoid, and the creation of additional tribal consultation requirements. 
--

SB24-212 helps communities assess, site, and permit utility-scale renewable energy. Colorado Parks and Wildlife is directed to publish maps of habitats vulnerable to renewable development and to update best management practices for projects in those areas. In the Brunot Treaty area, local governments must consult with Tribal Nations whose hunting, fishing, and gathering rights are tied to those lands.
To support local capacity, the bill directs the Colorado Energy Office to build a model regulations repository that communities can draw from, and tasks the Colorado Energy and Carbon Management Commission with providing technical assistance to governments updating land use codes or reviewing specific projects.
EO_Leg_ID: SB24-212 - Local Govs Renewable Energy Projects

action_tool_name: Providing Additional Permit Denial Information
reform_category: Promote Certainty in Permitting Process
description: SB 447 Act requires that denials to land disturbance or building permits be issued in writing with citations to applicable law or code, and that local governments publish clear criteria for what constitutes a complete application.

action_tool_name: Creating Faster Permitting Processes
reform_category: Consolidate or Eliminate Steps,Promote Certainty in Permitting Process
description:
- (1) Agencies must halve discretionary permit review timelines for qualifying housing projects.
- (2) Permits are automatically deemed approved if agencies miss statutory or regulatory deadlines.
- (3) All agencies must prioritize housing permit applications above all other applications.
- (4) Concurrent permit processing is required across agencies.
- (8) Monthly reporting metrics to the Governor on permit processing times.

action_tool_name: Establishing Surface Mine Reclamation Permit Fees
reform_category: Benefits for Nature/Communities
description: WA Chapter 326, 2025 Laws (Establishing Surface Mine Reclamation Permit Fees) updates the permit fee structure for surface mine reclamation to increase the funding into the surface mining reclamation program account. It increases the fees paid for revisions of reclamation units and has reduced rates for public works project mines under seven disturbed acres.

action_tool_name: Streamlining the conversion of commercial buildings to residential
reform_category: Change Decision-Making Level,Consolidate or Eliminate Steps
description:
Under a 2025 law, commercial building with high vacancy rates (50%+) can undergo a "summary review" for conversion to residential, prohibiting extensive public hearings. Projects must still meet local building/fire codes.

Planning and zoning commissions must act within 65 days of receiving the application, with potential for, though not mandatory, expedited, non-discretionary review.

action_tool_name: Removing the Private-Law Barrier of Homeowners Association Covenants for Internal Accessory Dwelling Unit (ADU) Siting without Requiring a Zoning Change or Permit Approval.
reform_category: Consolidate or Eliminate Steps,Promote Certainty in Permitting Process,Change Decision-Making Level
description: Chapter 265 (2023 laws) made it illegal for homeowner associations to ban accessory dwelling units (ADUs) so long as the ADU was physically attached to an owner-occupied home.

action_tool_name: Establishing a Host Community Benefit Program
reform_category: Benefits for Nature/Communities
description:
New York’s **Host Community Benefit Program **guarantees that residents in municipalities hosting large-scale (25 MW or larger) renewable energy facilities receive direct annual credits on their electric utility bills for the first 10 years of the project's operation. 

The program is supported by renewable energy developers pay annual fees that fund utility bill credits for residential customers in host communities.

action_tool_name: Establishing a Permitting Concierge Service
reform_category: Set Projects up for Success,Shift Culture
description:
The flagship **Client Concierge Service** celebrated its fifth anniversary in April 2025 as DEEP's "front door" for complex permitting assistance. The service provides extra assistance to projects that are either complex or viewed as priority to agency and state leadership. These projects are particularly challenging**00% participant satisfaction** in better understanding DEEP's permitting process, with **85% rating the experience as "good" or "excellent"** and 15% neutral—no negative ratings.

 Testimonials praised the service as "best-in-class experience...should be a best management tool across all different AHJs/State agencies" and noted DEEP as the "first [agency] that provides this service...truly interested in protecting the environment while helping with progress."

In 2024 alone, the Concierge Team assisted **129 applicants** through pre-application meetings, saving participants an average of **four months** compared to projects proceeding without Concierge assistance.

action_tool_name: Preventing Conditionality on Approval of Building or Plumbing Permits
reform_category: Shift Culture
description: Florida Chapter 2026-62 prohibits a municipality from requiring an onsite sewage treatment and disposal system (OSTDS) construction permit as a condition for a single-family residence building permit. If proof is provided that the OSTDS construction permit has been applied for, the building permit may be issued.

action_tool_name: Enforcing Minimum Lot Sizes for Residential Development
reform_category: Consolidate or Eliminate Steps,Change Decision-Making Level,Promote Certainty in Permitting Process
description: Chapter 385 (Public Laws, 2025) lowered the maximum lot size municipalities may require for residential development to 5,000 square feet in areas served by public water and sewer, and increased the number of dwelling units municipalities must allow per lot from two to three, and four in designated growth areas or lots with public water and sewer.

action_tool_name: Adjusting Standards and Rules
reform_category: Consolidate or Eliminate Steps,Shift Culture
description:
- (5) Permit fees may be reduced or deferred.
- (6) Wetland buffer zones in growth areas reduced from 50 feet to 25 feet for Class II wetlands.
- (7) Builders may choose between 2020 or 2024 Residential Building Energy Standards.

action_tool_name: Prioritizing Permit Review and Related Clean Energy Project Approvals
reform_category: Shift Culture,Set Projects up for Success
action_tool_type: Reorganization or Creation of New Government Offices,Regulatory Change
description: WA EO 25-11 (Prioritize and Expedite Clean Energy Permit Reviews) forms the Joint Clean Energy Acceleration Team (JCEAT) that identifies and addresses barriers to planned projects’ ability to qualify for federal clean energy tax credits. Agencies outlined in this order are directed to prioritize the review of applications of any solar or wind energy project identified by the JCEAT. The Energy Facility Site Evaluation Council is  also directed to expedite the review of any actions required to begin construction of projects and the Utilities And Transportation Commission is directed to expedite the review of any new or pending request for proposals to procure clean energy resources potentially eligible for tax credits.

action_tool_name: Virginia Permitting Transparancy
reform_category: Utilize Data and Technology,Shift Culture,Promote Certainty in Permitting Process
description: This First-in-the-nation government-wide tracking platform, launched under Governor Youngkin, allows the public (and government) to track the status of all pending permit applications online.

action_tool_name: Evaluating Barriers to Grid Interconnection Processes for Clean Energy Projects
reform_category: Promote Certainty in Permitting Process,Utilize Data and Technology,Set Projects up for Success
description: Prior to Executive Order 25-29, no state agency held a formal charge to systematically document interconnection barriers across permitting, construction, and grid queue processes in Oregon. The EO directed Oregon Department of Energy to assess obstacles preventing large-scale clean energy projects from completing interconnection, including lengthy utility queue studies, misaligned federal and state review timelines, and unclear cost allocation for grid upgrades. ODOE opened a public comment period in early 2026 drawing input from developers, utilities, tribes, and local governments; Public Utilities Commission simultaneously integrated EO 25-29 priorities into interconnection docket Utility Miscellaneous 2417. The resulting report, due September 1, 2026, does not itself modify any rule or process but is intended to provide a stakeholder-validated baseline for agency and legislative action in 2027.

action_tool_name: Prohibiting local governments from requiring a zone change or conditional use permit for affordable housing on eligible sites within urban growth boundaries.
reform_category: Consolidate or Eliminate Steps,Set Projects up for Success,Promote Certainty in Permitting Process,Change Decision-Making Level
description: Prior to Chapter 385 Laws (2021), affordable housing development on non-residentially zoned land within urban growth boundaries required a zone change or conditional use permit, a discretionary local approval process with variable timelines and outcomes. Chapter 385 Laws (2021) eliminated that requirement by statute for qualifying projects, converting a discretionary local decision into a ministerial one governed by statewide standards. The bill also extended this protection to land owned by religious corporations. All 56 affected jurisdictions had complied by end of 2022; subsequent amendments in 2023 and 2024 expanded its application further.

action_tool_name: Online Building Permit System
reform_category: Utilize Data and Technology,Promote Certainty in Permitting Process
description: The Online Building Permit System was developed by the DSPS to allow municipalities to gain compliance with [2015 Act 211](https://docs.legis.wisconsin.gov/2015/related/acts/211). Not all municipalities utilize the Online Building Permit System.

action_tool_name: KY EEC Online eForms Permitting Portal
reform_category: Utilize Data and Technology,Promote Certainty in Permitting Process
description: Kentucky's Energy and Environment Cabinet (EEC) electronic submittals, including permits and registrations, are handled through the [Kentucky EEC eForms Portal](https://dep.gateway.ky.gov/eForms). The system streamlines the permitting and compliance process, allowing you to submit forms, track application status, and view pending or completed tasks online. [[1](https://eec.ky.gov/Environmental-Protection/Pages/services.aspx), [2](https://dep.gateway.ky.gov/eForms), [3](https://dep.gateway.ky.gov/eportal/)]
project_type: General,Clean Energy,Ecological Restoration,Water,Mining and Critical Minerals

action_tool_name: Permitting Enhancement and Evaluation Platform (PEEP)
reform_category: Utilize Data and Technology,Shift Culture,Promote Certainty in Permitting Process
description:
DEQ's Permitting Tracking Platform (and a precursor to the state-wide VPT platform). PEEP provides public schedules and tracking information for every environmental permit (and at a greater level of detail than VPT).

As a result of the dashboard, the department says it has sped up processing times by 65%.

action_tool_name: Establishing streamlined process for ecological restoration projects
reform_category: Consolidate or Eliminate Steps
description: Simplifies the permitting process for qualifying ecological restoration projects.

action_tool_name: Requiring "by-right" multifamily zoning in communities served by the Massachusetts Bay Transportation Authority
reform_category: Consolidate or Eliminate Steps,Change Decision-Making Level
description: Requires communities served by MBTA adopt zoning ordinances that allow for at least one multifamily district with a minimum required gross density of 15 units per acre.

action_tool_name: Reducing voting threshold required to approve changes to zoning ordinances
reform_category: Change Decision-Making Level
description: Previously, two-thirds of a local city council or town had to support changes to zoning ordinances. Legislation in 2020 now allows changes to be implemented with a simple majority.

action_tool_name: Standardizing Compatibility Requirements for Development Permits
reform_category: Shift Culture,Promote Certainty in Permitting Process
description: Florida Chapter 2026-7 standardizes development permit applications and compatibility considerations. It requires the amount charged for a development permit application be related to the cost of giving a final disposition of the application; requires local governments' development regulations to include methods to assess compatibility of allowable residential uses ; requires that a denial of a development permit on incompatibility grounds specify each area of incompatibility and allows local governments' to issue development permits contingent on incompatibility mitigation measurements.

action_tool_name: Strengthening Requirements for First Home Investment Zones
reform_category: Shift Culture,Set Projects up for Success,Promote Certainty in Permitting Process
description:
The First Home Investment Zone program, which is a legislative initiative enacted by S.B. 268 to combat Utah's housing affordability crisis, was created in a prior session. Chapter 440, (2035 laws) tightens its parameters. The core change is numerical, and density, affordability, and owner-occupancy thresholds are specified in statute for the first time.
The practical effect is to make zone qualification more predictable for municipalities and developers while reducing the risk of zones being proposed that do not deliver the intended housing outcomes.

action_tool_name: Restricting Issuance of Water Use Permits to Data Centers
reform_category: Promote Certainty in Permitting Process
description:
Florida Chapter 2026-65 provides that  a water management district (WMD) or the Department of Environmental Protection (DEP) may not grant a Consumptive Use Permit (CUP) to a large-scale data center if the proposed use of the water is harmful to the water resources of the area or is prohibited by the applicable local government. It also provides that the WMD or DEP may require the large-scale data center to use some portion of reclaimed water, in lieu of surface or groundwater in some cases, and prohibits large-scale data center CUP application approvals without a hearing 
Consumptive use is any use of water which reduces the supply from which it is withdrawn or diverted. A CUP establishes the duration and type of water use as well as the maximum quantity of water that may be withdrawn.

action_tool_name: Requiring Guideline Transparency for the Coastal Area Management Act
reform_category: Promote Certainty in Permitting Process
description:
**Add New Procedural Requirements for Coastal Area Management Act (CAMA) Guidelines**

The NC Chamber is aware of potential “verbal or unwritten” policy guidelines in the CAMA program. This section would require that CAMA post all guidelines on their website and increase transparency in the CAMA permitting process for the business community in coastal North Carolina.

action_tool_name: Speeding Up Certification Process for Dredging, Energy, Fuel, and Infrastructure Projects
reform_category: Promote Certainty in Permitting Process
description:
**Permitting Efficiency Reform for 401 Certifications to speed up deployment of dredging, energy, fuel, and infrastructure projects.**

This section will speed up 401 Certifications with a 60-day deemed approved shot clock for projects without a public hearing, and 90 days with projects requiring a public hearing. The NC Chamber is proud to support this section to speed-up deployment of critical pipeline infrastructure for fuels and future natural gas pipelines. This provision will aid MVP Southgate’s future project in North Carolina.

action_tool_name: Creating a Permitting Fast-track Program for Priority Energy Projects
Expediting permitting for certain energy projects
reform_category: Consolidate or Eliminate Steps,Promote Certainty in Permitting Process,Shift Culture
description:
- The bill creates a new expedited certificate process for certain dispatchable energy generation projects and energy storage devices, replacing some of the usual CPCN timing and review steps with a faster, prioritized process.
- It also creates an expedited certificate process for qualifying projects, including a requirement that the Commission prioritize these proceedings and that other state agencies waive or expedite related regulatory requirements to meet the statutory timelines.
- For some projects, the Commission may approve a project first and review decommissioning plans later, so permitting can move faster without waiting for every downstream detail to be completed up front.
- The bill creates a separate application-and-approval path for nuclear energy generation projects after the Commission adopts implementing regulations.
project_type: Clean Energy,Housing and Other Building Construction,Data Centers

action_tool_name: Improving Efficiency of Water Quality Permitting
reform_category: Consolidate or Eliminate Steps,Promote Certainty in Permitting Process
description:
SB25-305 make a number of changes to water infrastructure permitting in CO:
- Limits the CDPHE's review of a permit‑modification application to only the specific changes requested, rather than reopening the entire permit, as was the previous standard.
- Directs the Water Quality Control Commission to adopt rules by December 31, 2026, allowing a 14-day public review of a preliminary draft permit before formal public notice
- Requires and sets deadlines for the CDPHE to propose, and the Water Quality Control Commission to adopt, rules setting clear time frames by which the division must grant or deny each type of permitting action (CDPHE's deadline is December 31, 2027, the Commission's deadline is June 30, 2028).
- Allows an applicant, after a permit modification or renewal application has been pending for 60 days (or if the division declines to process preliminary effluent limits), to jointly hire an independent contractor under division oversight to provide technical assistance to move the permit action forward, with costs paid by the applicant plus a small administrative fee to the state.
- Requires the CDPHE, on request, to provide a permittee with all non‑privileged documents, data, and information it relied on when developing that permittee’s permit modification or renewal.

action_tool_name: Allowing revenue sharing arrangements
reform_category: Benefits for Nature/Communities
description: Allows cities and towns to distribute revenue from housing. development to community members.

action_tool_name: Speeding up energy permitting
reform_category: Shift Culture,Consolidate or Eliminate Steps,Promote Certainty in Permitting Process,Benefits for Nature/Communities
description:
- It recommends a “Critical Infrastructure Fast-Track” designation for projects that meet Smart Siting criteria, so executive agencies can prioritize permit review and process permits concurrently.
- The order directs agencies, working with PSC staff, to propose fast-track permitting workflows for projects sited on state-owned rights-of-way.
- Those workflows are supposed to keep strict environmental protections and community engagement requirements in place.
- The order also asks the workgroup to identify state-owned corridors and other properties that could support transmission and co-located infrastructure, which is meant to make siting and permitting easier.

action_tool_name: Investing in Improved Permitting Technology
reform_category: Utilize Data and Technology,Shift Culture,Building the Permitting Workforce
description: In 2024, Governor Josh Shapiro and the Pennsylvania General Assembly approved $7 million for technology updates and upgrades in the 2024-25 state budget, which is meant to be used to increase transparency for permit applicants and residents and improve DEP’s record keeping systems.

action_tool_name: Requiring Most Municipal Governments to Adopt Automated Permitting for Residential Solar
reform_category: Utilize Data and Technology
description:
HB25-1096:
- Requires all counties and municipalities with 5,000+ residents to adopt an automated residential solar permitting platform that automatically reviews and issues permits for residential solar installations, and prohibits manual review of applications that the software approves, excepting physical inspections. 
- In addition, the State Electrical Board must implement its own automated electrical permitting platform for residential solar.

HB23-1234:
- Creates the "Streamlined Solar Permitting and Inspection Grant Program" in the Colorado Energy Office (CEO)
- Provides $992,709 in grant funding to help local governments adopt automated permitting software ("SolarAPP+") for residential solar projects
EO_Leg_ID: HB25-1096 - Automated Permits for Clean Energy Technology,HB23-1234 - Streamlined Solar Permitting And Inspection Grants

action_tool_name: Establishing an Office Focused on Improving PA's Digital Services
reform_category: Utilize Data and Technology,Shift Culture
action_tool_type: Reorganization or Creation of New Government Offices
description: In 2023, Governor Shapiro established the Commonwealth Office of Digital Experience (CODE PA) with the goal of improving, streamlining, and continuously adapting PA digital services so that “interactions between users and commonwealth entities are simple, seamless, and secure.” The office has been instrumental in the state's deployment of innovative technology tools such as PAyBACK, e-permitting, and more.

action_tool_name: Establishing the Infrastructure Strike Team and Developing a California Project Tracker
reform_category: Change Decision-Making Level,Consolidate or Eliminate Steps,Utilize Data and Technology,Promote Certainty in Permitting Process
description:
EO N-8-23's Infrastructure Strike Team is a formal mechanism that created an interagency structure with an explicit mandate to coordinate permitting review for large infrastructure projects and identify opportunities for it. 
The Strike Team mandate includes identifying duplicative federal, state, tribal, and local review requirements and developing strategies to reduce or coordinate them. 
This EO directs the development of a California-specific project tracking system to provide public transparency and accountability on permit status for critical infrastructure projects.

action_tool_name: Establishing a Permitting Process for Nature Based Methods to Improve Coastal Relisiliency
reform_category: Shift Culture,Benefits for Nature/Communities
description:
Florida Chapter 2026-1 requires the Department of Environmental Protection (DEP) to initiate rulemaking to establish a statewide permitting process for nature based methods to improve coastal resiliency. This includes erecting living shorelines and vegetation planting, seagrass planting, wave attenuation devices, and green or hybrid green-gray stormwater infrastructure, which are sited to provide the most appropriate benefit. 

Resilience is the ability of a community to prepare for anticipated natural hazards, adapt to changing conditions, and withstand and recover rapidly from disruptions.

action_tool_name: Considering EJ communities in permitting
reform_category: Benefits for Nature/Communities,Shift Culture
description: Requiring a person applying for a certain permit from the Department of the Environment to include in the application the EJ Score from the Maryland EJ tool for the census tract where the applicant is seeking the permit; requiring the Department, on receiving a certain permit application, to review the EJ Score for the census tract where the applicant is seeking a permit using the Maryland EJ tool to verify the applicant's information

action_tool_name: Speeding Up Transit-Oriented Development
reform_category: Consolidate or Eliminate Steps,Promote Certainty in Permitting Process
description:
HB24-1313 contained several provisions intended to speed up the approval process of housing projects near CO transit centers:
- Creates "transit-oriented community" (TOC) designation for jurisdictions meeting population and transit thresholds 
- Mandates administrative approval for multifamily residential development on parcels of 5 acres or less in transit centers 
- Prohibits "moving the goalposts" by mandating that once an application is submitted, local governments cannot change the rules or standards used to review it
- Limits grounds for denial to objective infrastructure standards (transportation, utilities, public works)
- Prohibits planned unit development restrictions from being more restrictive than underlying local law in transit centers 
- Creates TOC Infrastructure Fund grant program for certified TOCs; communities failing to comply face state injunction authority 
- Requires CDOT to study policy barriers to context-sensitive design in transit corridors
- Allows water-based permit conditioning to remain in place — local governments can still deny or condition approvals based on water supply, so it doesn't override infrastructure-based permitting standards.

action_tool_name: Standardizing Permitting for Land Use and Free Speech
reform_category: Change Decision-Making Level,Promote Certainty in Permitting Process
description: [HB 2019](https://www.azleg.gov/legtext/56leg/1R/bills/HB2019S.pdf) (2023, “Permit Freedom Act”) requires municipalities and counties that issue permits or licenses for free speech/assembly activities or activities changing land use, appearance, or density to publish clear, objective criteria for approvals. It mandates that local jurisdictions approve or deny such permits within 60 days of an application being deemed administratively complete, with applications automatically deemed approved if the deadline is missed (subject to some exceptions), and allows judicial review if governments impose conditions beyond their authority. By imposing uniform state-level standards and timelines on local permitting in defined categories, it functionally elevates state control over key aspects of permitting and zoning-related processes.

action_tool_name: Requiring Permit Application Status Websites
reform_category: Utilize Data and Technology
description: Creates a new section within Georgia's Erosion and Sedimentation Act (O.C.G.A § 36-60-34) requiring all local governments that process 250+ permits per year to create a publicly accessible website to post real-time permit status updates by January 1, 2028.

action_tool_name: Requiring consideration of environmental justice impacts in environmental review
reform_category: Benefits for Nature/Communities
description: 2023 legislation required agencies reviewing actions under the states environmental quality review act (SEQR) to evaluate whether an action may cause or increase a disproportionate pollution burden on disadvantaged communities (DACs).

action_tool_name: Creating a tool to identify EJ communities
reform_category: Benefits for Nature/Communities
description: MassEnviroScreen is an interactive map intended to identify the most environmentally vulnerable or burdened communities in Massachusetts. It is intended to support the guidance and regulations on cumulative impact analysis and site suitability assessments.

action_tool_name: Electronic Permit Submission
reform_category: Utilize Data and Technology,Set Projects up for Success
description: DEP allows [applications and supplemental materials to be submitted online or by mail.](https://www.pa.gov/services/dep/track-an-environmental-permit-application)

action_tool_name: Minnesota Online Permit Application Portal
reform_category: Utilize Data and Technology,Promote Certainty in Permitting Process
description: MPCA online services give users online access to apply for permits and licenses, submit required information, and pay fees and invoices.

action_tool_name: Streamlining Permitting for EV Charging Systems
reform_category: Promote Certainty in Permitting Process
description:
Per HB24-1173, on or before December 31, 2025, the board of county commissioners or the governing body of any non-exempt county of a covered municipality was required to do one of the following:
- Adopt an ordinance or resolution that incorporates either identical or less restrictive permitting processes and standards than those of the Colorado Energy Office's EV charger permitting model
- Develop and enact its own objective standards and administrative review process for EV charger permit applications, or:
- Declare by ordinance or resolution that it does not intend to adopt the state's EV charger model code or the Act's required standards and review process

Whether a county adopts its own standards or opts out, the state (and applicants) are now aware of which framework applies to them and the permitting strategies of their municipality.
EO_Leg_ID: HB24-1173 - Electric Vehicle Charging System Permits

action_tool_name: Ending Single-Family-Only Zoning
reform_category: Change Decision-Making Level,Promote Certainty in Permitting Process,Consolidate or Eliminate Steps
description: A 2022 law requires municipalities that currently cap density at 1 or 2 units per lot to allow more housing. On a vacant lot, a municipality must permit up to 2 dwelling units as a baseline, rising to up to 4 units if the lot sits in a designated growth area or is served by public water and sewer in a town without a comprehensive plan. On a lot that already has one dwelling unit, the municipality must allow up to 2 additional units—one inside or attached to the existing structure and one detached (or one of either).

action_tool_name: Moving permitting approaches from reactive to proactive
reform_category: Shift Culture,Consolidate or Eliminate Steps,Set Projects up for Success,Promote Certainty in Permitting Process
description:
- The order says Maryland should shift from “reactive permitting to proactive deployment” by creating a Site-Readiness Initiative for energy projects.
- It directs agencies to identify previously disturbed land that may be suitable for rapid energy deployment and to do preliminary environmental and interconnection feasibility assessments for priority sites.
- It calls for a “Pre-Application Consultation” process so developers can learn what permits may be needed and what environmental hurdles may arise before filing a CPCN application.

action_tool_name: Platform for Sharing Capital Improvement Plans Statewide (MiDIG)
reform_category: Utilize Data and Technology
description: The Michigan Infrastructure Council Project Portal (MiDIG) provides local governments and agencies a centralized, searchable online database to submit and track infrastructure asset management data and capital improvement projects.

action_tool_name: Updating and Streamlining Industrial and Clean Energy Permits
reform_category: Consolidate or Eliminate Steps,Promote Certainty in Permitting Process
description:
A 2025 law updated and streamlined the permitting process for industrial, wind, and solar energy projects. Key changes include:
- Updated deadlines for siting applications, including extending the county hearing deadline for wind and solar facilities from 60 to 90 days after completeness determination
- Setting timelines for applicants to cure incomplete applications.

action_tool_name: Compensatory Mitigation Explainer
reform_category: Benefits for Nature/Communities
description: Explainer on how compensatory mitigation works in PA.

action_tool_name: Allowing Accessory Dwelling Units By-Right
reform_category: Consolidate or Eliminate Steps,Promote Certainty in Permitting Process,Set Projects up for Success
description:
Chapter 672 (Public Laws, 2021) established a statewide by-right entitlement for Accessory Dwelling Units (ADUs). This meant that a property owner on a single-family lot could build one ADU without planning board approval, additional parking, or meeting dimensional requirements that are more restrictive than that of the primary dwelling. 
Local planning boards retain authority over building code and structural compliance but not over the land use decision.

action_tool_name: Requiring Environmental Analysis in EJ Communities
reform_category: Benefits for Nature/Communities
description: Requires an Environmental Impact Report for any proposed project located within one mile of an EJ Population, or located within five miles of an EJ Population if air quality will be impacted.

action_tool_name: Defining Residential Parking Stall in State Code and Prohibiting Garage Requirements for Qualifying Affordable Owner-Occupied Homes
reform_category: Consolidate or Eliminate Steps,Promote Certainty in Permitting Process,Set Projects up for Success
description:
SB 181 defines a residential parking stall in state code for the first time and prohibits local governments from requiring garages as a condition of building permit approval for deed-restricted, owner-occupied homes priced at or below 80% of the county median home price. 
Local governments retain authority to require on-site parking, but may not impose garage requirements for qualifying affordable units in the state's 90 largest cities and specified counties.

action_tool_name: Mandating Adherence to State Standardized Solar Siting
reform_category: Promote Certainty in Permitting Process,Change Decision-Making Level
description: Embeds detailed siting and zoning criteria in 55 ILCS 5/5‑12020, including fixed setback distances from residences and roads, maximum allowable sound levels tied to Illinois Pollution Control Board rules, and standards for things like height and fencing. Then, the Act specifies that county standards “may not be more restrictive than” these statutory requirements, turning permit review into a compliance check against a uniform statewide baseline.

action_tool_name: Allowing the Commissioner for Energy and the Environment to extend some permits beyond their expiration date
reform_category: Consolidate or Eliminate Steps,Promote Certainty in Permitting Process
description: A 2025 CT Law allows the CT Commissioner for Energy and the Environment to extend the validity of general permits beyond their expiration date until a new permit is issued or a decision is made not to renew, provided that the commissioner gives notice of intent to renew at least 180 days prior to expiration. It also removes the previous requirement for a final decision on renewal to be made within twelve months after expiration, thereby offering greater flexibility.

action_tool_name: Appointing a Chief Customer Experience Officer
reform_category: Shift Culture,Utilize Data and Technology
description:
Creates the Chief Customer Experience Officer position to improve data-driven service delivery, standardize agency website design, increase access to public benefits, and expedite processing times.

The current office holder, Chief Tonya Webster is the first Governor-appointed Chief Customer Experience Officer in the country and has more than two decades of executive experience leading customer experience transformations for governments and the private sector.

action_tool_name: Online Portal for Department of Environmental Protection Permitting
reform_category: Utilize Data and Technology
description: The Department of Environmental Protection Offers a Comprehensive E-permitting Dashboard to apply for and view progress of permits across all its offices.

action_tool_name: Raising Mandatory Energy Facility Siting Council Jurisdictional Thresholds + Expanding County-level Permitting for Solar Facilities
reform_category: Consolidate or Eliminate Steps,Change Decision-Making Level,Promote Certainty in Permitting Process,Set Projects up for Success
description:
Chapter 503 (2024 Laws) raised the acreage thresholds at which Energy Facility Siting Council jurisdiction becomes mandatory for solar facilities from 160 to 240 acres on high-value farmland. It expanded county-level permitting as the default pathway for mid-sized projects. 
Developers below the new thresholds may choose county review or opt into Energy Facility Siting Council review depending on project needs. The bill also closed a secondary barrier by requiring the Oregon Department of Transportation and counties to treat renewable energy right-of-way requests the same as utility requests.

action_tool_name: Housing Density Overhaul in Transit-Oriented Areas
reform_category: Change Decision-Making Level,Consolidate or Eliminate Steps,Promote Certainty in Permitting Process,Set Projects up for Success
description:
Chapter 512 (2025 Statutes), also known as the Abundant and Affordable Homes Near Transit Act, eliminates local zoning as a barrier to housing development near transit. This law overrules height limits, density caps, and single-family zones within half a mile of qualifying stops. 
This law expands eligibility for SB 423 ministerial review at lower affordability thresholds, meaning many SB 79 projects will qualify for CEQA-exempt ministerial approval. 
The practical significance is greatest in the Bay Area, where virtually all BART and Caltrain stations qualify as Tier 1 transit stops, and where single-family zoning adjacent to transit stations has historically prevented dense residential development despite high demand.

action_tool_name: Permitting for Wastewater Treatment Systems
reform_category: Consolidate or Eliminate Steps,Promote Certainty in Permitting Process
description:
2025 Legislation creates new rules for licensing, permitting, and inspecting wastewater treatment (septic) systems, with the Department of Environmental Quality (DEQ) having exclusive authority to adopt and enforce these regulations. 

Key provisions include: 
- Mandating that individuals must be licensed to install septic systems (with an exception for homeowners working on their own property), establishing standards for system installation and inspection, and creating a process for appealing permit decisions. 
- The bill empowers the DEQ to issue licenses, set technical standards, and impose civil penalties for violations, with penalties ranging from $5,000 to $12,500 depending on the nature of the infraction. 
- Public health units will be required to conduct inspections within one business day of receiving a request and can enter cooperative agreements with local governments for these services. 
- The legislation also appropriates $99,500 to the DEQ for implementing these new regulations and requires the department to provide a report to the legislative management by August 1, 2026. 
- Additionally, the bill repeals previous sections of code related to the onsite wastewater recycling technical committee, streamlining the regulatory approach to these systems.

action_tool_name: Reorganizing Permitting Appeals Bodies
reform_category: Shift Culture,Change Decision-Making Level
action_tool_type: Reorganization or Creation of New Government Offices
description: Eliminated the Environmental Permit Review Commission (EPRC) and the Environmental Review and Reuse Commission (ERRC), which were administrative bodies that heard appeals of EGLE permitting decisions. Their functions were transferred to other parts of state government, removing these intermediate appeal bodies from the permitting process and streamlining the appeals pathway.

action_tool_name: CT ezFile Portal
reform_category: Utilize Data and Technology
description:
ezFile can currently be used for:
- Stormwater Registrations (Construction, Industrial and No Exposure)
- Underground Storage Tank Notifications
- Boating and Fishing Permits and Registrations
- Radiation (DTX and RMI)
- Air Emissions Inventory and Compliance Reporting (EMIT)
- Waste Transportation (Hazardous Waste, Non-Hazardous Waste, Biomedical Waste and Emergency Responder/Spill)
- Aquatic Pesticide Permitting (New Permit Applications and Annual Re-Introductions)
- Coastal Permit Applications (Certificate of Permission and Structures, Dredging & Fill / Tidal Wetlands)
- Natural Diversity Data Base Review Requests (NDDB Requests) and Site Assessments

Built on Microsoft .NET with integrations to IBM Enterprise Content Management, ESRI GIS mapping, and Melissa address verification, ezFile provides 24/7 availability, real-time processing, pre-populated dropdown menus, electronic fee payment, and template functionality for repeat applications.

action_tool_name: Creating the Housing Accountability and Production Office
reform_category: Change Decision-Making Level,Set Projects up for Success,Shift Culture,Building the Permitting Workforce
description: Before Chapter 110 (2024 Laws), developers encountering unresponsive local permitting had no formal state-level recourse outside of Land Use Board of Appeals proceedings. The Housing Accountability and Production Office (HAPO) filled that gap by creating a dedicated state office empowered to receive developer complaints and engage directly with local governments on housing production compliance.

action_tool_name: Incorporating Kit Homes into Regulatory Framework
reform_category: Promote Certainty in Permitting Process
description: WA Chapter 41, 2026 Laws (Concerning the Creation of Building Codes for Kit Homes) establishes a new category of building codes specifically for kit-homes, explicitly defines kits homes and instructs the State Building Code Council to develop rules for them by March 31, 2027.

action_tool_name: Requiring Cumulative Impact Assessments for Permits in EJ Communities
reform_category: Benefits for Nature/Communities
description: Under CT's 2023 Environmental Justice law, the Department of Energy & Environmental Protection (DEEP) can develop regulations that will enable new **[cumulative impacts](https://portal.ct.gov/deep/environmental-justice/environmental-justice-law-regulations-development)** assessments. The new change allows DEEP to impose permit conditions or even deny permits to applicants seeking expansion or construction of their facilities.

action_tool_name: Standardizing Permitting for All Housing
reform_category: Promote Certainty in Permitting Process
description: The 2025 legislative session of Washington took action to standardize building permits in counties and cities planning under the Growth Management Act (GMA). One law prohibits cities planning under the GMA from requiring a 'change of use' permit for buildings in residential zones being converted to residential use and makes unchanged portions of a building that were used for residential purposes and permitted exempt from current Energy Code requirements. Another law clarifies the definition of project permit to explicitly exclude building permits, this is intended to make application requirements and deadlines clearer for all applicants and cities planning under the GMA.

action_tool_name: Environmental Permitting Tracker
reform_category: Utilize Data and Technology,Shift Culture,Promote Certainty in Permitting Process
description: PA DEP now allows the public to track the status of any new permit application. The tracker provides an estimated completion date for each step in the review process.

action_tool_name: Creating an Office to Drive Regulatory Modernization
reform_category: Change Decision-Making Level,Eliminate Permits/Permitting,Consolidate or Eliminate Steps
action_tool_type: Reorganization or Creation of New Government Offices,Regulatory Change
description:
Created in June 2022, the Office of Regulatory Management has drove the Governor's regulatory modernization agenda. According to the Youngkin Administration, the Office's efforts reduced 50,000 regulatory requirements.

ORM was also responsible for the development of VPT, the statewide permit tracking platform.

We have not been able to determine the status of this office under Governor Spanberger.

action_tool_name: Establishing the ExpressNY Initiative
reform_category: Shift Culture
description: Establishes a Statewide effort to identify and eliminate outdated regulations and streamline government operations, with an initial agency review identifying nearly 100 regulations for reform or elimination. Includes public portal for citizen submissions and appointment of an Associate Director of Regulatory Reform in the Executive Chamber, targeting housing/infrastructure development, small business requirements, and service delivery processes.

action_tool_name: Establishing the Streamlining Permitting, Efficiency and Economy Development (SPEED) Council
reform_category: Change Decision-Making Level,Consolidate or Eliminate Steps,Promote Certainty in Permitting Process,Building the Permitting Workforce
action_tool_type: Regulatory Change,Reorganization or Creation of New Government Offices
description: A 2025 executive order by Governor Little established the Streamlining Permitting, Efficiency, and Economic Development (SPEED) Council. The SPEED Council is both responsible for coordinating the permitting of major economic development projects in the state and for streamlining permitting more broadly.

action_tool_name: Eliminating off-street parking requirements for smaller housing developments
reform_category: Change Decision-Making Level
description:
HB 8002, signed in 2025 eliminates off-street parking requirements for housing developments with less than 16 units.

A municipality may impose minimum parking requirements on residential developments exceeding 16 dwelling units, but developers must be permitted to submit a "parking needs assessment." The approving commission or zoning officer — shifting authority back to the municipality — must then cap parking as a condition of approval at no more than one space per studio or one-bedroom unit and two spaces per larger unit, or at whatever number the needs assessment recommends, whichever yields the lower off-street parking requirement.

action_tool_name: Standardizing the review of new rules and regulations (including for permitting)
reform_category: Eliminate Permits/Permitting,Consolidate or Eliminate Steps,Promote Certainty in Permitting Process
description: Under a 2022 law, every KS agency that issues rules and regulations must review all of its adopted rules on a five-year cycle. For each one, the agency must tell the Joint Committee whether the rule is still necessary to implement and administer state law, or whether it can be revoked. The bill also created a streamlined process for revoking any rule the review flags as no longer needed.

action_tool_name: Removing the Requirement to Adopt Comprehensive Zoning Ordinances
reform_category: Shift Culture
description: West Virginia Chapter 65, Acts, Regular Session, 2024 removes the requirement for counties to draft and adopt comprehensive zoning ordinances in their development plans.

action_tool_name: Resources for Applicants Navigating State Environmental Permitting
reform_category: Set Projects up for Success
description: EGLE offers one-on-one compliance and permitting assistance to businesses, municipalities, and developers through a dedicated hotline (800-662-9278), personalized permitting checklists (including for farms and schools), and a library of guidance documents, fact sheets, and training workshops.
project_type: General,Other,Clean Energy,Housing and Other Building Construction

action_tool_name: Establishing a One Stop Permitting Process
reform_category: Change Decision-Making Level,Utilize Data and Technology
description: West Virginia Chapter 134, Acts, Regular Session, 2025 instructs the secretary of the West Virginia Department of Administration to lead the various permitting agencies in the implementation of a One-Stop-Shop Permitting Program - a dashboard for obtaining and renewing all permits. It also outlines information on permitting that all relevant  agencies are required to share with the secretary and requires alternative paper-based application with no penalty for users.
EO_Leg_ID: West Virginia Chapter 134, Acts, Regular Session, 2025

action_tool_name: Reinstating and Improving the Energy Security Task Force
reform_category: Change Decision-Making Level
action_tool_type: Reorganization or Creation of New Government Offices
description:
Reinstates the Alaska Energy Security Task Force with a revised membership and reporting structure, comprised of 15 members including commissioners from DEC and DNR, the Alaska Energy Authority, and chaired by the Lieutenant Governor. 

The Task Force was directed to assess Alaska's current energy portfolio, identify risks and opportunities, and develop solutions centered on affordability, reliability, and security. It was also tasked with recommending policies, regulatory changes, funding strategies, a public energy data portal, and a statewide energy goal with an actionable plan to achieve it.
EO_Leg_ID: Administrative Order No. 345 - Alaska Energy Security Task Force

action_tool_name: Prioritizing Efficient Use of Administrative Resources
reform_category: Shift Culture,Change Decision-Making Level
action_tool_type: Regulatory Change,Reorganization or Creation of New Government Offices
description: WA Chapter 98, 2026 Laws (Improving Efficiency in Appeals to the Pollution Control Hearings Board) allows appeals of environmental permits to be heard by alternative arrangements of the Pollutions Control Hearing Board ( PCHB) if both the appealing party and the PCHB consent.

action_tool_name: Expanding the Use of General Permits
reform_category: Promote Certainty in Permitting Process
description:
Florida Chapter 2025-165 grants a general permit for the replacement of an existing onsite sewage treatment and disposal system with a Distributed Wastewater Treatment System (DWTS) / Unit (DWTU) if it is commonly owned and operated by the permittee.

A general permit is a permit issued by rule of the DEP. A person may proceed under a general permit 30 days after notifying the DEP, without any further action by the DEP.

action_tool_name: Creating a Temporary Voluntary County-level Siting Pathway for Qualifying Renewable Energy Facilities
reform_category: Consolidate or Eliminate Steps,Change Decision-Making Level,Promote Certainty in Permitting Process,Set Projects up for Success
description:
Chapter 12, (2026 Laws) removed the Energy Facility Siting Council (EFSC) site certificate requirement for qualifying renewable energy facilities, allowing developers to seek county-level land use approval instead. 
This bypassed a process that historically took one to three years for projects needing to meet federal tax credit construction deadlines. 
The county pathway is voluntary for both developers and host jurisdictions, and all other local permitting requirements remain in place.

action_tool_name: Ensuring Residential Development in Qualifying Parcels
reform_category: Shift Culture,Promote Certainty in Permitting Process
description:
Florida Chapter 2026-84 provides that a local government must permit a qualifying parcel to be developed with residential uses. The proposed development of a qualifying parcel must be administratively approved, and no further action by the governing body of a local government is required. 

Qualifying parcels are plots of land adjacent to other areas zoned for residential use. They must also be environmentally impacted, meaning contaminants or pollutants have been detected on the land above certain thresholds or the land has been designated a brownfield area.

action_tool_name: Permitting Consolidation and Institutional Realignment
reform_category: Shift Culture,Change Decision-Making Level
action_tool_type: Reorganization or Creation of New Government Offices,Regulatory Change
description:
- Replacing Minnesota’s Power Plant Siting Act (Minn. Stat. Ch. 216E) with a new “Minnesota Energy Infrastructure Permitting Act” (Minn. Stat. Ch. 216I) that consolidates permitting requirements for energy facilities currently found in multiple existing statutes and rules into one new chapter
- Moving the Environmental Review and Analysis Staff (EERA) from the Minnesota Department of Commerce to the Commission (effective July 1, 2025)

action_tool_name: Requiring Ministerial and CEQA-Exempt Approval of Affordable Housing on Religious and Higher Education Lands
reform_category: Consolidate or Eliminate Steps,Change Decision-Making Level,Set Projects up for Success
description:
Chapter 781 (2023 Statutes) allows religious institutions and nonprofit colleges or universities to build affordable housing on their land by right and without rezoning, irrespective of the current zoning designation of the property. This law eliminates the rezoning and conditional use permit requirements, converting approval to a ministerial administrative process with a statutory timeline. 
In addition, the state law would override local zoning authority for this specific land use category, removing local discretion to deny qualifying affordable housing proposals on religious or educational institution land. 
By removing the zoning barrier upfront, faith and non-profit higher education institutions can proceed directly to building permits without prior land use approval.

action_tool_name: Expediting Permitting of Agricultural Enclaves
reform_category: Shift Culture,Promote Certainty in Permitting Process
description: Florida Chapter 2026-34 allows the owner of an agricultural enclave may apply for certification of the land as an agricultural enclave. If the local government does not approve or deny certification within 90 days, the parcel must be certified as by default. If the application is denied, the governing body of the local government must issue its decision in writing with detailed findings. It also prohibits a local government from enacting or enforcing a law or regulation for an agricultural enclave which is more burdensome than for other types of applications for comparable uses or densities.

action_tool_name: AccessDEQ Portal
reform_category: Utilize Data and Technology,Promote Certainty in Permitting Process
description: AccessDEQ is DEQ’s umbrella online platform and guidance hub that centralizes information on environmental permits, many of which can now be applied for and tracked electronically. It is being incrementally expanded to include more permit types and online workflows to reduce processing time and improve transparency.

action_tool_name: Increasing Efficiency and Predictability in the Processing of State issued permits
reform_category: Shift Culture,Consolidate or Eliminate Steps,Promote Certainty in Permitting Process,Utilize Data and Technology
description: WA EO 25-03 (Improving Transparency and Building Efficiency in the State's Permitting and Licensing processes) directs all permitting agencies to submit to the Office of the Governor information on their permitting processes. This includes data such as the types of permits, the methods with which applications are received, the authority governing the length of time in which the agency must process applications, the agency's analysis of the speed with which it could consistently process each type of permit and other relevant information. After consultation with the Office of the Governor each agency shall establish efficient and appropriate application processing deadlines for each type of permit and make the updated deadlines public

action_tool_name: Establishing comprehensive plans for zoning predictability
reform_category: Change Decision-Making Level,Consolidate or Eliminate Steps,Promote Certainty in Permitting Process
description:
- **Mandatory rezoning in designated areas.** If a city or village receives a rezoning request for residential development in an area identified in its comprehensive plan as projected for residential use, the municipality generally must grant the request within 60 days.
- **Least restrictive fallback.** If the local comprehensive plan does not meet the bill’s new requirements, the land must be rezoned to the requested classification or to the least restrictive zoning classification allowed.
- **New planning requirements.** Municipal comprehensive plans must identify where residential growth is expected and specify minimum and maximum density levels in five-year increments over a 20-year horizon.
- **Zoning must match planning.** The bill pushes cities and villages to make their zoning ordinances consistent with those comprehensive plans, so permitting decisions cannot diverge as easily from the plan.
- **More predictability for developers.** The practical permitting effect is to reduce case-by-case opposition and delay for residential projects that conform to the plan, because the approval path becomes more automatic.

action_tool_name: Establishing the Pro-Housing Communities Certification Program
reform_category: Shift Culture,Set Projects up for Success,Promote Certainty in Permitting Process
description: Creates a voluntary certification program recognizing municipalities that adopt pro-housing policies; localities must earn the certification to qualify for key grants including the Downtown Revitalization Initiative and Public Transportation Modernization Enhancement Program.

action_tool_name: Amending the Maximum Fee due with a Permit Application
reform_category: Promote Certainty in Permitting Process
description: SD 2025 Session Laws Chapter 188 (An Act to Amend the Maximum Fee Limit Due When Applying for a County Drainage Permit) requires any board or commission to increase the maximum cost of a permit fee for applicants for a drainage board from $100 to $500 and clarifies that this fee is paid with the application.

action_tool_name: Requiring Pre-Application Evaluation of Data Center Water Usage
reform_category: Set Projects up for Success
description:
Data Center developers in Minnesota must now report to the MN DNR earlier in the planning process on how much water their project is projected to use.

Data centers that use more than 100 million gallons of water a year must consider using water conservation technology. This includes closed-loop systems that recycle water for cooling.

action_tool_name: Expediting Issuance of Development Permits
reform_category: Promote Certainty in Permitting Process,Set Projects up for Success,Shift Culture
description: Florida Chapter 2026-64 takes several actions to ensure the timely approval of development permits. It requires local government to offer pre-application consultation services ; give a final decision on applications within 45 days with partial refunds if no response is given in 30 days and automatic approval if there is no communication 10 days after the deadline.

action_tool_name: Increasing Efficiency in Division of Water Resources Processes
reform_category: Consolidate or Eliminate Steps
description:
A 2025 law on water well permitting did the following: 
- Extended well construction timeline from 1 year to 2 years after permit issuance
- Removed obsolete permitting steps and requirements for non-Denver Basin bedrock aquifer wells in designated basins
- Eliminated final permitting requirements for certain non-Denver Basin bedrock wells
- Streamlined the beneficial use determination process for water wells
EO_Leg_ID: HB25-1014 - Increasing Efficiency Division of Water Resources

action_tool_name: Bypassing Discretionary Review and CEQA for Duplexes and Lot-Splits on Single-Family Zoned Parcels
reform_category: Consolidate or Eliminate Steps,Promote Certainty in Permitting Process,Change Decision-Making Level
description:
Chapter 162 (2021 Statutes) converted California's existing prohibition on duplexes and lot-splits into a ministerial entitlement. 
The state law overrides local single-family zoning authority, which removes local discretion to deny qualifying applications on the basis of use or density. 
A property owner meeting the eligibility criteria can submit an application to rezone their parcel, and the local agency must approve it without a public hearing or California Environmental Quality Act (CEQA) review.

action_tool_name: Updating Requirements for Carbon Capture Facilities
reform_category: Promote Certainty in Permitting Process
description:
West Virginia Chapter 133, Acts, Regular Session, 2024 amends the permitting of Carbon Dioxide Capture Sequestration and Storage (CCS) facilities by requiring that facilities meet requirements of their Class VI underground injection control permits including requirements related to post-injection site care and site closure to be issued a certificate of completion. It also only allows issuance of the certificate 50 years after injections end ( up from 10 years ) or other site-specific timeframe established with the legislative rules.

CCS facilities separate CO₂ from industrial emissions and inject it deep underground for permanent disposal.

action_tool_name: Creating a Permitting Fast-track Program for Priority Energy Projects
reform_category: Consolidate or Eliminate Steps,Shift Culture
description: One of the key areas of Executive Order 18 is to support energy production in Delaware. The Order aims to create a "Priority Energy Project Fast-track" that coordinates review across various agencies. This coordinated review aims to reduce permit processing times well below quoted averages of 18-24 months. The Order specifically refers to clearing backlogs for solar, battery storage, clean energy, and nuclear projects.

- [Governor's Office Press Release](https://news.delaware.gov/2026/02/26/governor-matt-meyer-signs-executive-order-streamlining-state-permitting-regulations/)
- [EO FAQ ](https://www.dropbox.com/scl/fi/e8rde5jivy7g1k2ph0ndf/Permitting-Accelerator-FAQ.pdf?rlkey=y09rctvcyo6oxxf4w6pxkac3u&st=q9yo9444&dl=0)
- [EO Implementation Guidelines](https://governor.delaware.gov/wp-content/uploads/sites/219/2026/02/1043_001.pdf)
EO_Leg_ID: Executive Order #18 - Implementing the Delaware Permitting Accelerator

action_tool_name: Establishing new requirements for watershed-level compensatory mitigation
reform_category: Benefits for Nature/Communities
description:
The Commissioner of Energy and Environmental Protection may attach a condition to certain permits and water quality certifications requiring "watershed-level compensatory mitigation" — meaning steps taken to offset harm done to water resources elsewhere in the same watershed. This condition may be imposed in two situations: first, when the permitted activity is carried out by a state department, agency, or instrumentality (excluding local and regional boards of education); and second, when the permitted activity takes place within the public trust, such as in inland wetlands, watercourses, tidal wetlands, or coastal waters.
The permits and certifications to which this authority applies are those issued under Connecticut's wetlands, dam safety, and coastal management statutes, as well as federal water quality certifications issued under Section 401 of the Clean Water Act.

action_tool_name: Enhancing Permitting of Micro-Grids and Data Centers
reform_category: Set Projects up for Success
description: West Virginia Chapter 112, Acts, Regular Session, 2025 is a comprehensive act to spur development of microgrids and data centers. It creates the Certified Microgrid Program permitting the operation of microgrids to power high-impact data centers and mandates that certification be obtained from the Department of Commerce. It also instructs the department secretary to appoint a "Data Economy Liaison" to serve as a single point-of-contact for certified microgrid districts and prohibits county or municipal building permitting, inspection, or code enforcement on certified microgrids.

action_tool_name: Creating a Legislative Taskforce on Energy Generation Permitting
reform_category: Shift Culture
description: The Louisiana State Senate created a task force to study and make recommendations (by March 1) relative to polices that promote energy self-generation, industrial microgrids, and expedited permitting in the state of Louisiana.

action_tool_name: Modifying the Riparian Buffer and Floodplain Rules for Airport Development
reform_category: Consolidate or Eliminate Steps
description:
**Modify the Riparian Buffer and Floodplain rules for airports to encourage investment and expansion in our world-class airport infrastructure.**

This section will aid the RDU Airport Authority in their runway expansion and ensure our critical airports can continue to grow, expand, and compete.

action_tool_name: DCM CAMA e-permitting
reform_category: Utilize Data and Technology,Set Projects up for Success,Promote Certainty in Permitting Process
description: The Division of Coastal Management’s online system supports major permits, general permits, and enforcement/compliance actions under the Coastal Area Management Act (CAMA), allowing applicants to apply, track status, and manage modifications electronically. DEQ describes it as part of a broader initiative to modernize environmental permitting and make interactions more efficient.

action_tool_name: Establishing an Office for Supporting Businesses and Project Sponsors in the Permitting Process
reform_category: Set Projects up for Success,Promote Certainty in Permitting Process,Shift Culture
action_tool_type: Reorganization or Creation of New Government Offices
description: [The Office of Transformation and Opportunity](https://www.pa.gov/agencies/oto) acts as a dedicated concierge for businesses and project sponsors navigating state permitting across multiple agencies, coordinating meetings and helping sponsors understand requirements from the start.

action_tool_name: Urban Growth Boundary Expansion Tool
reform_category: Promote Certainty in Permitting Process,Set Projects up for Success,Consolidate or Eliminate Steps,Change Decision-Making Level
description: Chapter 110 of the 2024 Oregon Laws introduced Urban Growth Boundary expansions, previously administered through the Land Conservation and Development Commission's standard amendment process. This was a multi-step, discretionary proceeding with variable timelines. SB 1537 created a one-time alternative tool allowing eligible cities to expand their Urban Growth Boundaries outside that process, conditioned on 30% affordability requirements in expansion areas. Practical impact depends on uptake by individual cities and their capacity to meet the affordability condition.

action_tool_name: Creating regulations for the Siting and Colocation of Wireless Infrastructure
reform_category: Promote Certainty in Permitting Process
description: West Virginia Chapter 42, Acts, Regular Session, 2025 creates new regulations for the siting and colocation (installing) of wireless infrastructure. Siting authorities are mandated to approve applications within a certain period of time with automatic approvals if they are unable to. It also requires automatic approval of modifications to wireless infrastructure that fits a certain criteria and prohibits authorities from imposing recurring unreasonable fees.

action_tool_name: Reviewing Existing Regulations to Identify and Eliminate Inefficiencies
reform_category: Consolidate or Eliminate Steps
description:
Administrative Order 360 seeks to promote growth and investment in Alaska by reducing administrative and economic burdens associated with regulatory compliance by streamlining permitting processes and improving agency coordination.
