The Administrative Order specifically mentions (but is not exclusive to) Alaska DNR, DEC, and DFG to review their permitting processes and reduce the number of regulatory requirements by 15 percent by December 31, 2026, and 25 percent, cumulative, by December 31, 2027.

action_tool_name: Rebuilding I-95 in 12 Days
reform_category: Shift Culture,Change Decision-Making Level,Consolidate or Eliminate Steps,Eliminate Permits/Permitting
description: When a critical section of I-95 collapsed in Philadelphia in June 2023, Pennsylvania Governor Josh Shapiro issued a disaster declaration, which empowered agencies to side step the traditional regulatory process and accelerated permitting, procurement, and contracting—authorizing actions that normally take months or years to be completed in days. As a result, I-95 reopened to traffic just 12 days after the disaster, showcasing how proactive executive leadership that consolidates or eliminates steps in the permitting process can bypass bureaucratic inertia and deliver results for the public.

action_tool_name: Establishing a Port Permitting Pathway for Offshore Wind Procurement
reform_category: Change Decision-Making Level,Utilize Data and Technology,Promote Certainty in Permitting Process,Set Projects up for Success
description:
The Maine Legislature created a statutory framework for port permitting within Title 35-A of the Maine Revised Statutes, directing the Maine Department of Transportation to identify a preferred port site and manage the siting process. This function created the Sears Island designation in 2024, a 100 acre portion of state-owned land that became a port facility. 
Environmental reviews of project impacts on fisheries, wildlife, and the broader marine ecosystem are coordinated through the Maine Department of Energy Resources.
The Maine Offshore Wind Research Consortium is directed to produce data on floating wind technology performance and ecosystem impact, providing an evidence base for subsequent siting and permitting decisions.

action_tool_name: Expediting Issuance of Building Permits
reform_category: Shift Culture,Set Projects up for Success,Building the Permitting Workforce
description:
2025 and 2026 FL expedited the issuance of building permits.
One law prohibits local governments from requiring copies of contracts & associated documents for building permits and reduces the time for a local building department to review a permit for a single-trade plan\* for a single or two-family dwelling. 
Another law creates an expedited building permit program and prevents local governments from imposing various conditions on applicants. It also requires governments to subsidize staff resources with qualified contractors for plat approvals if requested.
\*A single-trade plan is a plan focused on a single construction trade such as plumbing, mechanical, or electrical. It is needed to ensure compliance with the building code other applicable municipality requirements.

action_tool_name: Establishing a Regulatory Sandbox
reform_category: Change Decision-Making Level,Promote Certainty in Permitting Process
action_tool_type: Reorganization or Creation of New Government Offices,Regulatory Change
description: A 2025 law creates a new Regulatory Relief Division within the Kansas Attorney General's office to establish a General Regulatory Sandbox Program, which allows businesses to temporarily test innovative offerings by obtaining limited waivers or suspensions of certain state rules and regulations. The program enables businesses to demonstrate new products or services in a controlled environment with consumer protections, providing a pathway for companies to test potentially groundbreaking ideas that might otherwise be restricted by existing regulations. Participating businesses must apply through a detailed process that involves consultation with relevant state agencies and an advisory committee, and must disclose potential risks to consumers. Each sandbox participant can operate under modified regulatory requirements for up to 24 months, with the possibility of a 12-month extension, while being required to maintain rigorous reporting standards and consumer safeguards.

action_tool_name: Creating a consolidated local permit for small clean energy projects
reform_category: Eliminate Permits/Permitting,Consolidate or Eliminate Steps
description: New regulations borne from the 2024 Climate Act allowed the Department of Energy Resources to create a new, consolidated local permitting process for small clean energy projects (under 25 MW for generation, under 100 MWh for storage)

action_tool_name: MDEQ Online Permit Portal
reform_category: Utilize Data and Technology,Promote Certainty in Permitting Process,Set Projects up for Success
description: The Environmental Permits Division (EPD) implements and oversees most of the permitting programs for MDEQ. EPD also provides support to the Environmental Compliance and Enforcement Division (ECED) in regulating the permitted facilities.

action_tool_name: Establishing 48-Business-Hour Inspection Timeline with a Third-Party Inspection Fallback and Virtual Re-Inspections
reform_category: Consolidate or Eliminate Steps,Promote Certainty in Permitting Process,Change Decision-Making Level,Utilize Data and Technology
description: This bill amends the Idaho Building Code Act and introduces a 48-Business-Hour time limit on building inspections, and an option to involve a third-party inspector in the case of a delay. The virtual re-inspection provision adds a parallel efficiency mechanism that reduces the need for return site visits for other corrections. This, this legislation addresses the bottlenecks of initial scheduling delay for inspections, as well as repeat physical visit requirements.

action_tool_name: Centralizing Rulemaking Power Under the Governor
reform_category: Shift Culture,Change Decision-Making Level
description:
EO 23-02 bolsters the Governor's authority by requiring Governor pre-approval for any proposed new rule or amendment to an existing rule before a state agency may present it to a legislative committee. In addition, the order applies to all existing pending rules, and for every one new rule an agency proposes, it must simultaneously submit two existing rules for repeal. 

This Executive Order's intention is stated to reduce regulatory burden which may hinder economic development initiatives.
EO_Leg_ID: EO 23-02 - Reduce Government Rules and Regulations

action_tool_name: Requiring Large Data Centers Pay for Low-income Home Weatherization and Energy Conservation
reform_category: Benefits for Nature/Communities
description: Large Data Centers are now required to pay between $2 and $5 Million a year into a state program that helps low-income Minnesotans weatherize their homes and conserve energy.

action_tool_name: Creating a State Housing Ombudsman
reform_category: Promote Certainty in Permitting Process,Building the Permitting Workforce
action_tool_type: Reorganization or Creation of New Government Offices
description:
- The Department of Housing and Community Development must designate a State Housing Ombudsman to help developers and other stakeholders navigate local, state, and federal permitting processes.
- The ombudsman also serves as a liaison among state agencies, local governments, planning and zoning authorities, developers, and communities.
- That role includes gathering information on local permitting, planning, and zoning processes, identifying bottlenecks, and tracking housing projects’ progress.
EO_Leg_ID: EO 01.01.2025.19 / ADDRESSING MARYLAND'S AFFORDABLE HOUSING CRISIS

action_tool_name: Clarifying Requirements and Deadlines for Zoning Approval
reform_category: Shift Culture,Promote Certainty in Permitting Process
description: Florida Chapter 2025-177 is a comprehensive act that creates uniform standards for how local governments process zoning permits. It requires local governments to specify in writing the minimum information that must be submitted in an application for a zoning approval, rezoning approval, subdivision approval, certification, special exception, or variance. It also revises timeframes for processing applications for development permits or development orders and mandates local governments to issue refunds to applicants if they fail to meet certain deadlines when processing an application .

action_tool_name: Facilitating siting of one recreational vehicle on rural residential-zoned parcels as an additional dwelling unit.
reform_category: Consolidate or Eliminate Steps,Change Decision-Making Level,Promote Certainty in Permitting Process
description: Before Chapter 295, (2023 Laws) rural residential zoning limited parcels to a single dwelling per ten acres. SB 1013 amended that framework to permit one recreational vehicle as an additional dwelling unit on qualifying rural residential parcels, without requiring a zone change or state-level discretionary approval. Implementation authority was delegated to counties, which may impose registration, rental agreement, and inspection requirements, meaning practical availability of the use varies by jurisdiction.

action_tool_name: Directing appeals of local siting decisions to the state siting board instead of the courts
reform_category: Change Decision-Making Level
description: Requires appeals of local decisions on consolidated local permits (a tool created by the 2024 Climate Act) to go directly to the state siting board, bypassing the courts.

action_tool_name: Allowing Qualified 3rd Party Reviewers for Environmental Permits
reform_category: Building the Permitting Workforce,Shift Culture,Promote Certainty in Permitting Process
description: Created in July 2024 through the bipartisan 2024-25 state budget, SPEED allows qualified third-party professionals (verified by DEP) to conduct the initial technical review of certain permit applications (air quality, earth disturbance, dam safety, individual water obstruction/encroachment, and more). DEP staff then review the QP's recommendation and make the final permit decision, significantly accelerating timelines.

action_tool_name: Enforcing Permitting of Qualified Residential Property in Commercial Zones
reform_category: Shift Culture,Promote Certainty in Permitting Process
description: WA Chapter 236, 2026 Laws (Concerning Residential Development in Commercial and Mixed-Use Zones) bill prohibits cities and counties planning under the Growth Management Act from excluding residential uses in areas zoned for commercial or mixed-use development. It also establishes an administrative waiver process for ground-floor commercial requirements in residential buildings and supersedes conflicting local rules.

action_tool_name: Inventorying and Cutting Regulations (and Permits)
reform_category: Eliminate Permits/Permitting,Consolidate or Eliminate Steps
description: EO 735 Instructed all State Agencies to prepare an inventory of existing administrative rules (including permits). The Agencies were then instructed to develop a written plan to streamline its administrative code, and to begin implementation of these plans.

action_tool_name: Establishing the Office of Renewable Energy Siting and Electric Transmission
reform_category: Change Decision-Making Level
action_tool_type: Reorganization or Creation of New Government Offices,Regulatory Change
description: Establishes the ORES, replacing the standard Article 10 siting process for renewable energy projects and transmission lines.

action_tool_name: Creating Permitting Process for Carbon Capture and Storage
reform_category: Change Decision-Making Level
description:
AK HB 50 creates a two-step permit process and designates DNR as the agency to issue carbon storage exploration licenses and AOGCC as the primary permitting authority for CCS. 

The Act also expands the Regulatory Commission of Alaska's jurisdiction to natural gas and LNG storage facilities.
EO_Leg_ID: HB 50 - Carbon Storage, Cook Inlet Oil and Gas

action_tool_name: Studying the impact of data centers on electricity prices
reform_category: Shift Culture
description: A 2025 NJ law requires the Board of Public Utilities (BPU) to conduct a comprehensive study on how data centers impact electricity costs in New Jersey within one year of the bill's effective date. The BPU must then submit a report of its findings to the Governor and Legislature within 15 months of the bill's effective date.

action_tool_name: Updating Provisions in the Permit Application Process for Energy and Transmission Facilities
reform_category: Shift Culture,Promote Certainty in Permitting Process,Consolidate or Eliminate Steps
description: SD 2026 Legislative Session SB 25 (An Act to Update Provisions Related to the Permit Application Process for Proposed Energy and Transmission Facilities) specifies timelines for applicants of proposed energy facilities to inform adjacent property owners and members of the public. It also mandates that the Public Utilities Commission, which as part of its permitting review process can form a voluntary review committee of local stakeholders, notifies members of the committee within 10 days . The committee is dissolved if at least 3 members do not participate, and their local review is not considered by the commission in the issuance of a permit.

action_tool_name: Enhancing Permitting Tools
reform_category: Shift Culture
description: WA Chapter 166, 2026 Laws (Providing for Enhanced Municipal Permitting Tools for High Capacity Transit Projects) enhances the permitting process for high-capacity transit projects by allowing regional transit authorities to apply for land use and construction permits before acquiring property and to apply for construction permits before a final land use decision is made.

action_tool_name: Requiring Municipalities to Report Annual Residential Permit Statuses to the State Government
reform_category: Promote Certainty in Permitting Process,Utilize Data and Technology,Shift Culture
description:
Chapter 495 (Public Laws, 2025) requires municipalities to standardize and collect residential permit data and send it to the Department of Economic and Community Development. The DECD then publishes the data on housing permits given, demolitions, and status updates on other housing-related permits such that there is state-managed data infrastructure.
This standardization of residential permitting in a publicly accessible dataset creates a baseline against which state agencies, municipalities, and developers alike can measure housing production, and identify permitting bottlenecks or compliance failures.

action_tool_name: Mandating and Expediting Permitting Timelines
reform_category: Promote Certainty in Permitting Process
description: The Act requires state agencies and local governments to process permits for “Energy Infrastructure Projects” (EIPs) (a project entailing the construction, placement, authorization, or removal of energy infrastructure, as referred to by SCDES) on an explicit schedule of six months from receipt of the application. If the permit has not been issued within six months, or within any mutually agreed extended deadline, the application is deemed approved by law.
EO_Leg_ID: A41, R60, H3309 - Energy Security Act

action_tool_name: Mandating Statewide Accessory Dwelling Unit Allowance by Right on All Single-family Lots
reform_category: Change Decision-Making Level,Consolidate or Eliminate Steps,Set Projects up for Success,Promote Certainty in Permitting Process
description:
A 2025 law (Chapter 149, 2025 laws) established that every single-family lot in Iowa is legally entitled to at least one accessory dwelling unit (ADU) by right, subject only to reasonable local design and dimensional standards that do not function as a de facto prohibition.
As a result, ADU approvals are no longer at the discretion of local government.

action_tool_name: Streamlining Wind and Solar Siting Standards
reform_category: Change Decision-Making Level,Consolidate or Eliminate Steps,Promote Certainty in Permitting Process
description:
The CRGA tightens and clarifies 55 ILCS 5/5‑12020 so counties follow a uniform process for wind and solar siting, instead of navigating varying local standards and processes.
- **Core process and timing rules**
    - Counties that choose to regulate must hold at least one public hearing, and the hearing must conclude within 60 days after the application for a commercial wind or solar facility is filed.
    - The county must make its siting and permitting decision no later than 30 days after the hearing concludes, and it cannot impose a shorter construction‑start window than the five‑year period established in statute.
- **Standardized siting and fencing treatment**
    - The statute codifies setback tables and expressly allows solar perimeter fencing without treating the fence itself as triggering larger setbacks; counties may only impose “reasonable” additional rights‑of‑way setbacks consistent with the statutory scheme.
    - Vegetative screening requirements are constrained by specifying maximum initial heights (five feet at installation) and by narrowing the circumstances under which outside wildlife consultation (such as with the U.S. Fish & Wildlife Service) can be demanded as a condition of local siting approval.
- **​Fee caps and “presumptively reasonable” charges**
    - Application fees for county siting approval or special use permits for commercial wind and solar are deemed presumptively reasonable if they do not exceed the lesser of $5,000 per megawatt of nameplate capacity or $125,000 total.
    - County building permit fees for these facilities are likewise presumptively reasonable if they do not exceed the lesser of $5,000 per megawatt or $75,000 total, preventing localities from using open‑ended fee schedules as a de facto barrier to entry.
EO_Leg_ID: SB0025, 104th General Assembly - Clean and Reliable Grid Affordability Act

action_tool_name: Expanding the Definition of Energy Conversion Facility
reform_category: Promote Certainty in Permitting Process
description: SD Chapter 193, 2025 Laws (An Act to Include a Hybrid Facility as a Facility to be Regulated by the Public Utilities Commission) expands the definition of energy conversion facility to explicitly include hybrid facilities and encompasses them in the Public Utilities Commission's regulatory framework, including the issuance of permits. It defines hybrid facilities as a facility that has more than one type of generation source and has a single point of interconnection to the distribution or transmission system.

action_tool_name: CEQA-Exempt Approval of up to 10-Unit Housing on Multifamily Sites
reform_category: Consolidate or Eliminate Steps,Promote Certainty in Permitting Process,Set Projects up for Success
description:
Chapter 788 (2023 Statutes) requires CEQA-exempt ministerial approval of housing development projects of up to 10 units on qualifying multifamily infill sites less than or equal to five acres, zoned for multifamily residential use.
The project must be located in an urbanized area and not involve the demolition of existing, occupied rental housing in order to be eligible. With these factors considered, this law allows developers to gain access to a streamlined pathway for mid-sized housing options that previously lacked a clear permitting route under previous laws.

action_tool_name: Imposing a Moratorium on New Administrative Rulemaking and Directed Comprehensive Review of All Existing Agency Rules
reform_category: Change Decision-Making Level,Consolidate or Eliminate Steps,Promote Certainty in Permitting Process,Shift Culture
description:
Executive Order 10 imposed a statewide pause on new administrative rulemaking while requiring all agencies to justify the continued existence of existing rules through a phased review process. 
This Order creates the mechanism through which agency-administered permit rules may be rescinded. The moratorium's primary near-term effect is regulatory stability, wherein no new administrative burdens may be added to permitting processes during the review period. 
Longer-term impact depends on the volume and nature of rule rescissions produced by agency reviews, which have not been comprehensively reported as of June 2026.

action_tool_name: Establishing Small Business Environmental Support Office
reform_category: Set Projects up for Success
action_tool_type: Reorganization or Creation of New Government Offices
description: Establishes a new office to assist small businesses in navigating existing renewable energy, clean air and water, and environmental justice regulations and programs.

action_tool_name: Encouraging Permanent Supportive Housing, Transitional Housing, Indoor Emergency Housing, and Indoor Emergency Shelters
reform_category: Shift Culture,Set Projects up for Success
description: WA Chapter 232, 2026 Laws (Encouraging Permanent Supportive Housing, Transitional Housing, Indoor Emergency Housing, and Indoor Emergency Shelters) requires cities and counties to allow emergency shelters, transitional housing, emergency housing, and permanent supportive housing (STEP) in zones where residential units or hotels are already permitted. It also prohibits them from being located in industrial zones.

action_tool_name: Authorizing the Department of Environmental Protection to Put New Regulations Into Effect
reform_category: Shift Culture
description: West Virginia Chapter 168, Acts, Regular Session, 2025 places into effect regulatory changes made by the Department of Environmental Protection (DEP) regarding the National Pollutant Discharge Elimination System (NPDES) Program. One change is that the DEP Director is required to re-issue permits before the termination of the existing permit. Another requires the Director to develop alternative permit limits and conditions for alternative operating scenarios or conditions that are reasonably likely to occur and are identified by the permit applicant, at the request of the permittee.

action_tool_name: Updating environmental permitting
reform_category: Benefits for Nature/Communities,Consolidate or Eliminate Steps
description: The bill directs the Department to analyze how **S**tate and local laws, permits, actions, and policies affect environmental justice communities. It also includes landfill-related regulatory changes, including methane standards and limited exemptions, but those are framed as regulations and exemptions rather than a broader permit redesign.

action_tool_name: Establishing Strategic Growth Plan and Ensuring Local Government Compliance
reform_category: Change Decision-Making Level,Shift Culture
description:
Governor Jared Polis issued a series of Executive Orders which aim to pressure local governments to allow more housing and denser development

EO D 2023 014:
- Established Colorado’s “strategic growth” objectives, primarily affordable housing and access to transit, and directs relevant agencies (including CDOT, CEO, DOLA, OEDIT) to inventory their own policies, plans, procedures, and rules that can support those objectives. Agencies are required to coordinate with each other and review their processes to identify and eliminate inefficiencies or possible delays.

EO D 2025 005: 
- Directs the Colorado Department of Local Affairs to determine and update each local government’s compliance with “strategic growth” land use and housing laws, and putting local governments on notice that compliance status will have important implications regarding discretionary funding. 

EO D 2025 011 
- Requires state government agencies to prioritize compliant local governments when awarding competitive grants across 34 funding programs, incentivizing local governments to adhere to state priorities outlined in the strategic growth objectives.
EO_Leg_ID: D 2025 005 - Regarding Strategic Growth through Compliance with State Laws,D 2023 014 - Concerning State Programs that Support Strategic Growth,D 2025 011 -  Amending and Supplementing Executive Order D 2025 005 Regarding Strategic Growth through Compliance with State Laws

action_tool_name: Enhancing Existing Permit Review Timelines for Land Disturbance Permits
reform_category: Promote Certainty in Permitting Process
description: SB 447 builds on the existing 45-day framework established for land disturbance permits in O.C.G.A. § 12-7-9 (Georgia's Erosion and Sedimentation Act) by making it stricter by adding deadlines for reviews and written denial requirements. Previously, the 45-day timeline would reset if the applicant submitted an incomplete or noncompliant plan. Under SB 447, the timeframe no longer resets, but instead sets 20 days for a second review upon receiving a new plan, then 14 days for all subsequent reviews, if needed.

action_tool_name: Establishing Energy Facilities Siting Board
reform_category: Change Decision-Making Level
action_tool_type: Reorganization or Creation of New Government Offices
description: Creates an independent, 11-person state board to review proposed clean energy infrastructure facilities including renewable generating facilities, energy storage, electric transmission lines; intra-state natural gas pipelines; fossil-fuel generating facilities; and natural gas storage tanks.

action_tool_name: Clarifying Discrepancy on Stormwater Permitting
reform_category: Promote Certainty in Permitting Process
description:
**Stormwater Program changes to remove discrepancy and encourage re-development.**

This section would clarify some discrepancies between The N.C. Department of Environmental Quality (NCDEQ) and the N.C. Department of Transportation (NCDOT) on stormwater permitting and grandfathered impervious surface regulations for existing developments. This provision will address these issues and speed up re-development of sites across the state.

action_tool_name: Establishing a self-certification program for ADUs
reform_category: Shift Culture,Change Decision-Making Level
description: WA Chapter 22, 2025 Laws (Establishing a self-certification program for accessory dwelling unit project permit applications) allows cities planning under the GMA to have registered architects engage in a self-certification program for building code certifications of accessory dwelling units (ADUs).

action_tool_name: Prohibiting Solar or Wind Bans/Moratoria
reform_category: Promote Certainty in Permitting Process,Change Decision-Making Level
description: The Act removes counties’ ability to use bans or moratoria as a permitting tool by prohibiting ordinances that “ban or unreasonably restrict” commercial wind or solar facilities that meet state siting criteria, so a county cannot avoid granting permits by declaring the use off‑limits across its jurisdiction.

action_tool_name: Updating school construction permitting
reform_category: Consolidate or Eliminate Steps,Promote Certainty in Permitting Process,Set Projects up for Success
description: For public school construction, the bill requires the Interagency Commission to establish a waiver process allowing a school system to be excused from net-zero or high-performance requirements when those requirements are not practicable, including where site limitations or cost make compliance infeasible. It also requires consultation on net-zero implementation and ties some requirements to funding availability through the Net-Zero School Grant Fund.
project_type: Housing and Other Building Construction,Clean Energy

action_tool_name: Documenting All State Permits and Creating a Money-Back Guarantee
reform_category: Shift Culture,Consolidate or Eliminate Steps,Set Projects up for Success,Promote Certainty in Permitting Process
description:
A 2026 executive order requires all state agencies under Governor Lamont’s authority to compile and submit to his office within a specific period a report containing a list of all licenses, certifications, and permits issued by the agencies and a summary of what they are and how applications are accepted (e.g. paper, email, web portal, or other). Among other information, the reports must also contain data on how many applications are received annually and how long on average it takes to process them. Additionally, agencies must propose a maximum number of days for applications to be completed for each individual license, permit, and certification.

Following receipt of the agency reports, the executive order requires the Office of the Governor to publish a list setting deadlines that each license, certification, and permit must be approved or denied. After publication of that list, if an agency has not completed processing applications within the designated amount of time required for each respective license, certification, and permit, applicants may request a refund of the application fee from the agency.

action_tool_name: Amending SB 174 Subdivision Review Framework and Clarifying Review Timelines for Small Municipalities and Reviewing Administrative Land Use Authority (ALUA) Designation Requirements
reform_category: Promote Certainty in Permitting Process,Consolidate or Eliminate Steps
description: HB 476 (2024) addresses implementation ambiguities in the SB 174 (2023) land use framework, primarily adjusting review timelines for small municipalities and clarifying the role of planning commissions in the two-step approval process. It does not introduce new substantive requirements.

action_tool_name: Removing State Approval for Developers in Brownfields Redevelopment Program
reform_category: Eliminate Permits/Permitting,Consolidate or Eliminate Steps,Change Decision-Making Level
description:
**Allowing developers working in the Brownfields Redevelopment Program to perform inspections and investigations without approval from the state to speed up redevelopment.**

Currently in the NC Brownfields Redevelopment Program (NCBP) the department is prohibiting developers from performing their own inspections on the front-end and instead must wait for the Department to approve them. This is delaying projects and stifling re-development projects in the state. This provision should speed up the process and the NC Chamber looks forward to seeking a greater reform of the NCBP statute in the future.

action_tool_name: Ensuring the right to use a Private Provider for Building Inspections
reform_category: Shift Culture
description:
Florida Chapter 2026-63 requires permit fees charged by local jurisdictions to reflect the actual cost incurred when an owner retains a private provider for building inspection services 
It also prohibits officials from charging punitive, administrative, or additional fees, including fees for building inspections or site review, when an owner uses a private provider and allows a property owner to choose to use a private provider at any time.

action_tool_name: Coordinating with the Federal Permitting Improvement Steering Council
reform_category: Change Decision-Making Level
action_tool_type: Reorganization or Creation of New Government Offices
description:
Here's a tightened version:
Alaska became the first state to sign an MOU with the Federal Permitting Council, creating a coordination structure between the state's Office of Project Management and Permitting (within DNR) and the Council to jointly identify projects eligible for FAST-41 coverage. FAST-41 is a voluntary federal program under Title 41 of the Fixing America's Surface Transportation Act that streamlines environmental reviews for major infrastructure projects. Coverage under FAST-41 is designed to provide:
- **Federal support:** assigned staff, regular coordination calls, a state-specific application template, and customized project tracking
- **Timeline predictability:** permitting schedules and public engagement opportunities posted on the Federal Permitting Dashboard at permitting.gov
- **Transparency projects:** a category that makes schedules and public comment opportunities publicly visible for accountability
- **Direct engagement:** the Council can brief project sponsors on FAST-41 processes
EO_Leg_ID: Memorandum of Understanding between The Federal Permitting Improvement Steering Council and The State of Alaska to Facilitate Implementation ofFAST-41 for Covered Projects and Transparency Projects

action_tool_name: Reforming Community Post-Storm Permitting
reform_category: Shift Culture,Promote Certainty in Permitting Process
description: FL Chapter 2025-190 is a comprehensive act that reforms permitting procedures for post-storm permitting. Counties are required to implement special procedures to expedite post-storm permits, prohibited from raising fees for 180 days if the Governor has declared a county in a state of emergency and need to ensure permitting staff are available as soon as is practicable. The intent of the act is "to accommodate the building of additional developments to ameliorate the acute affordable housing and building permit allocation shortage".

action_tool_name: Providing a Money-Back Guarantee for All State Permits, Licenses, and Certifications
reform_category: Promote Certainty in Permitting Process,Shift Culture
description:
Launched October 31, 2023, PAyBACK is an online money-back guarantee system for permit, license, and certification applicants. It publishes standard processing times for all ~2,400 permits, licenses, and certifications and allows applicants to request a refund if an agency misses its deadline. 

According to [PA DEP](https://www.pa.gov/agencies/dep/newsroom/2026-01-27-dep-achieved-historic-success-in-permitting-in-2025-eliminates-backlog), With more than 485,000 PAyBack eligible permits reviewed by DEP since 2022, DEP has never missed a deadline under the program.

action_tool_name: Streamlining Administrative Rulemaking and Reviewing Inefficiencies in Governance
reform_category: Shift Culture,Consolidate or Eliminate Steps,Utilize Data and Technology,Promote Certainty in Permitting Process
description:
In 2024, Governor Reynolds signed into law Chapter 414 (2024 laws, House File 2276) requiring cities to treat maternity group homes, residential facilities providing housing and support services for pregnant women and new mothers, as a permitted use by right in all residential zones, including single-family zones. 
The law also prohibits local governments from requiring a special use permit or rezoning for such facilities, and voids any deed restriction or covenant that would prohibit their establishment.

action_tool_name: Reviewing and Streamlining Housing Permits and Regulations
reform_category: Shift Culture,Consolidate or Eliminate Steps,Set Projects up for Success
description: On her first day in office, Governor Spanberger signed an executive order directing a comprehensive, multi-agency review of all regulations and permitting practices that affect housing development, with the goals of eliminating unnecessary requirements, streamlining approvals, and reducing barriers to housing production. The order establishes a Commission on Unlocking Housing Production to advise the Governor on strategies to increase housing supply, and directs it to recommend legislative, regulatory, and administrative changes to expand housing supply across Virginia.

action_tool_name: Standardizing County Hearing and Permit Decision Timelines
reform_category: Promote Certainty in Permitting Process,Change Decision-Making Level,Consolidate or Eliminate Steps
description: The Act standardizes permitting procedures by requiring the county board or zoning board of appeals to hold at least one public hearing “not more than 60 days after the filing of the application” and to make its siting or special use decision “not more than 30 days after the conclusion of the public hearing,” which constrains how long local governments can keep applications in limbo.

action_tool_name: Prohibiting Local Governments from Enforcing Construction Site Environmental Regulations More Restrictive than State and Federal Baseline Standards
reform_category: Consolidate or Eliminate Steps,Change Decision-Making Level,Promote Certainty in Permitting Process
description: In 2024, Governor Kim Reynolds signed Senate File 455, (Chapter 1011, 2024 laws). This law prohibits counties and cities from adopting or enforcing construction site regulations relating to topsoil preservation, compaction, placement, or depth that are more restrictive than the National Pollutant Discharge Elimination System General Permit No. 2 issued by the Iowa Department of Natural Resources. The law also restricts local stormwater regulations for new developments.

action_tool_name: Online Portal for Environmental Permits (myDEQ)
reform_category: Utilize Data and Technology,Shift Culture,Promote Certainty in Permitting Process
description:
MyDEQ is Arizona DEQ’s online portal that allows regulated entities to obtain, modify, and terminate various environmental permits and submit compliance reports electronically. First launched in 2016, in 2022 it expanded in response to public hearings.

Since its 2016 launch, it has rolled out at least 17 online services, [cutting average permit decision times across ADEQ from 121 days to about 8 days](https://www.nascio.org/wp-content/uploads/2020/09/NASCIO-2018-Submission-DEQ.pdf) and [generating over $100 million in estimated economic benefit](https://cei.org/studies/boosting-business-in-the-desert/).

action_tool_name: Creating Three Model Housing Zoning Ordinances Tailored to City Size
reform_category: Promote Certainty in Permitting Process,Consolidate or Eliminate Steps,Set Projects up for Success,Shift Culture
description: Chapter 111, 2024 laws directed local governments to develop their own zoning standards independently, producing variable approval frameworks across jurisdictions, such that an identical middle housing project could face different standards, timelines, and discretionary hurdles depending on location. The bill directed the Department of Land Conservation and Development to produce three model ordinances tailored to small, medium, and large cities. A city adopting the model ordinance receives a pre-built, state-vetted set of clear and objective development standards without commissioning its own rulemaking process. The Land Conservation and Development Commission adopted the model codes by January 1, 2026, with implementation guidance published in April 2026.

action_tool_name: Designating Points of Contact for Permit Applications
reform_category: Change Decision-Making Level,Shift Culture
action_tool_type: Reorganization or Creation of New Government Offices,Regulatory Change
description: WA Chapter 235, 2026 Laws (Concerning Permit Review Processes) requires local governments to designate an individual responsible for all final project permit decisions and to have a point of contact for each permit application.

action_tool_name: Ensuring Permitting of Manufactured Dwellings
reform_category: Shift Culture,Promote Certainty in Permitting Process
description:
The Florida 2026 Legislative Session took action to ensure permitting of manufactured housing. One law provides that a residential manufactured building certified by the Department of Business and Professional Regulation may be placed on any lot in a recreational vehicle park, instead of only those lots specifically designated for mobile homes. It also requires local governments to allow off-site constructed residential dwellings be permitted in any zoning district where single-family detached dwellings are allowed. 
Another law further clarifies that building permits may not be denied for the placement of manufactured housing on a recreational vehicle lot and requires offsite-constructed residential dwellings be treated the same as other residential single family detached dwellings.

action_tool_name: Reporting on Water Quality Permitting Timelines and Efficiency
reform_category: Shift Culture,Utilize Data and Technology,Promote Certainty in Permitting Process
description: The Department of Public Health and Environment's (CDPHE) is required to provide an annual report to the Water Quality Control Commission with data on how long the division takes to review and issue water quality permits, and how it is reducing the permit backlog and improving efficiency.

action_tool_name: Creating Governance Structures to Increase Permitting Support
reform_category: Shift Culture,Change Decision-Making Level,Promote Certainty in Permitting Process
action_tool_type: Reorganization or Creation of New Government Offices,Permit Cataloging/Mapping,Regulatory Change
description:
- Created the Land Use Review Board, a full-time professional board with expertise in planning, development, environmental science, and law.
- Created the Community Investment Board to allocate resources statewide.
- Tasked the Regional Planning Commissions with creating Future Land Use Maps through a public process.

action_tool_name: Boosting Housing and Development Projects for a Growing Population
reform_category: Change Decision-Making Level
description:
SB 382 - 
Creates the Montana Land Use Planning Act, overhauling the state's land use planning and how development projects are reviewed and permitted. States must adopt several state-developed plans, the Land Use Plan and Map, Zoning Regulations and Map, and Subdivision Regulations. Cities covered by the Plan and those who opt-in must assess their housing needs, land availability, rate of population growth, and identify obstacles to housing development, then adopt 5 of 14 state pro-growth strategies. 

Creates a review process for development projects which reduces requirements necessitating public hearings and comments, allowing for administrative approval for projects in compliance.  

Shifts public participation from the planning states and site-specific developments to just the planning and adoption of land use plans and codes. Individual development projects will be reviewed at the city-level and no longer require direct public involvement.

SB 406 - 
Create local state standards for building codes and prohibits local governments from creating stricter standards

action_tool_name: Ensuring Identical Permitting Requirements Under Different Ownership Structures
reform_category: Promote Certainty in Permitting Process
description: WA Chapter 208, 2025 Laws (Streamlining and Clarifying Local Governments' Land Use Permitting Workloads) ensures that condominium or cooperative forms of ownership face no more restrictive permitting than identical developments under other ownership structures.

action_tool_name: Exempting Routine Exterior Repairs and Replacements on Owner-Occupied Single-Family Homes from Local Building Permit Requirements
reform_category: Shift Culture,Eliminate Permits/Permitting
description: SD 2026 Legislative Session SB 3 (An Act to Limit the Types of Residential Improvements for Which Counties, Municipalities, and Townships May Require a Permit ) prohibits a county, municipality, or township from requiring a building permit for the repair or substantially similar replacement of various exterior parts of a detached single-family owner-occupied residential structure.

action_tool_name: Establishing an Endangered Species Mitigation Bank Fund
reform_category: Benefits for Nature/Communities
description: Establishes a mitigation bank fund as an alternative mechanism for clean energy and transmission project developers to meet endangered species mitigation requirements.

action_tool_name: Commercial Corridor CEQA Exemptions
reform_category: Change Decision-Making Level,Consolidate or Eliminate Steps,Promote Certainty in Permitting Process
description:
Chapter 647 (2022 Statutes) eliminates the rezoning requirement as a precondition for housing on commercial corridor sites, converting a two-step discretionary and then building the permit process into a single ministerial review.
The law creates a direct entitlement to housing on commercial sites meeting eligibility criteria, overriding local commercial zoning restrictions.
Moreover, through Chapter 647 (2022 Statutes) California has a ministerial pathway, such that a developer proposing housing on a qualifying commercial corridor site submits an application and the local agency must approve it ministerially within 90-180 days, without a public hearing and without CEQA.

action_tool_name: Granting Exceptions to Building Permits
reform_category: Shift Culture
description: Florida Chapter 2026-63 provides that a local government shall grant an exemption from a building permit for any work valued at less than $7,500 done on a single-family home and Temporary residential hurricane and flood protection walls that meet certain criteria.

action_tool_name: Enforcing Deadlines for Permit Approval
reform_category: Shift Culture,Promote Certainty in Permitting Process
description: West Virginia Chapter 70, Acts, Regular Session, 2024 requires local governments to respond to permit applications within 60 days and mandates automatic approval without further action if no response is given by the deadline. It also prohibits local governments from adding additional requirements after approving a permit and specify missing information if a permit is denied for incompleteness.

action_tool_name: Shielding projects from subsequent zoning changes
reform_category: Promote Certainty in Permitting Process
description: Protects from regulatory uncertainty by providing developers 3 year after a permit has been issued to begin construction, even if zoning amendments have changed the regulatory environment.

action_tool_name: Rolling Back MEPA Environmental Review
reform_category: Consolidate or Eliminate Steps
description:
Montana, over the course of a few years, significantly limited the power and scope of the MEPA environmental review process in efforts to accelerate permit issuance for development projects that would hopefully boost the state's economy. 

HB 85 - Expands categorical exclusions for agricultural and state land projects

HB 466 - Codifies categorical exclusions in statute and allows agencies to adopt federal categorical exclusions themselves

HB 285 - Declares MEPA reviews as informative and procedural, removing it's ability to be used as grounds for permit denial

HB 270 - Limits the court's ability to vacate permits for MEPA violations, providing that economic and public interest benefits weigh against said violations

SB 221 - Limits what agencies must analyze during MEPA to review to six discrete impacts, instead of the former analysis which was more open-ended.

action_tool_name: Streamlining and Expediting Energy Development
reform_category: Consolidate or Eliminate Steps,Promote Certainty in Permitting Process
description:
- Reducing the types of energy projects that must obtain a certificate of need (CN) from the Minnesota Public Utilities Commission (MPUC or Commission) (effective the day following enactment)
- Modernizing the Commission’s site and route permit processes into new categories: Major Review and Standard Review, reducing procedural steps and shortening the overall permitting timeline
- Shortening notice timelines for incumbent electric transmission owners under Minnesota’s Right of First Refusal requirements for transmission projects approved through the Midcontinent Independent System Operator (MISO) process

action_tool_name: Reforming Wastewater, Stormwater, and Disposal Systems
reform_category: Consolidate or Eliminate Steps,Promote Certainty in Permitting Process
description: Lets authorized on‑site wastewater evaluators issue site‑denial letters; modernizes wastewater permitting and modeling under a streamlined rulemaking path; creates permitting‑by‑regulation/general permits for qualifying non‑discharging disposal systems; limits repeated “second bite” stormwater permit reviews; and exempts small (1 acre) new residential projects from certain Falls Reservoir nutrient requirements.

action_tool_name: Requiring Cities to Select and Implement Specific Moderate-Income Housing Strategies from a State-Defined Menu, with Annual Reporting Obligations and Loss of Transportation Funding as the Consequence for Non-Compliance
reform_category: Shift Culture,Promote Certainty in Permitting Process,Set Projects up for Success,Change Decision-Making Level
description:
Chapter 406, (2022 laws) amended Utah's Moderate Income Housing requirements to mandate that cities select strategies from a state-defined list, which includes: rezoning for density reasons, creating houses near transit corridors, eliminating impact fees for accessory dwelling units, or allowing higher density near commercial areas. They must report progress annually to the state.
Cities that do not comply with the housing planning requirement lose eligibility for certain transportation funds. This is a financial penalty designed to incentivize compliance, not a transportation policy in itself.

action_tool_name: Map of Mitigation Bank Service Areas
reform_category: Utilize Data and Technology
description: Mitigation banking is a practice in which environmental preservation or enhancement is engaged in to mitigate unavoidable ecological impacts within a defined region. This map provides service areas for state approved mitigation banks.

action_tool_name: Standardize Permitting and Siting for Battery Storage
reform_category: Promote Certainty in Permitting Process,Change Decision-Making Level
description: CRGA establishes uniform county permitting standards for standalone energy storage systems. Permitting is tied to siting approvals, special-use permits, building permits, fees, setbacks from batteries, and delays in governmental approvals. This initiative furthers the CRGA mandated goal of procuring and deploying three gigawatts of battery storage by 2030 by increasing permitting certainty.
EO_Leg_ID: SB0025, 104th General Assembly - Clean and Reliable Grid Affordability Act

action_tool_name: e-Permitting for the Department of Environmental Protection
reform_category: Utilize Data and Technology
description: The Florida Department of Environmental Protection (DEP) has several portals that applicants may use to submit permits related to water and air resources. It also has a number of maps and dashboards that allows users to view various permits issued by the DEP, online wizards that guide users in the permit application progress - including sample documents-, and a document management system to search for any permit issued by the DEP.

action_tool_name: Permitting the Use of Biological Filtration Systems
reform_category: Promote Certainty in Permitting Process
description: SD 2026 Legislative Session HB 1299 (An Act to Permit the Use of Biological Filtration Systems in Water Recreational Facilities Located in Certain Lodging Establishments) authorizes lodging establishments that have fifteen or fewer sleeping rooms to apply to the Department of Health for a permit to use a biological filtration system to filter water and instructs the department to establish rules for applicants.

action_tool_name: Limiting Local Government Power to Deny Renewable Energy-Related Permits
reform_category: Change Decision-Making Level
description:
SB 159 prohibits and county from denying a conditional use permit for electrical substations, and any infrastructure which is directly related, when certain conditions are met: 

1) the substation is related to a renewable energy project that is at least 250 MW
2) the project is located on unincorporated county land 
3) the land is zoned for heavy industrial use
4) the project meets all applicable state and federal regulatory requirements. 

It also retroactively approves certain applications that were previously denied by any DE county. 

SB 199 reinforced SB 159 by removing the expiration date of these reforms, which was previously in 2027.

action_tool_name: Reforming Permitting around State Parks
reform_category: Shift Culture,Promote Certainty in Permitting Process
description: Florida Chapter 2025-76 (State Land Management) authorizes the Florida Department of Environmental Protection (DEP) to permit the installation of campsites and cabins in state parks within certain guidelines. It also prohibits the DEP from permitting sporting facilities or lodging establishments within state parks.

action_tool_name: ePermitting Portal for MI Environmental Permits
reform_category: Utilize Data and Technology
description: The Michigan Department of Great Lakes and the Environment launched MiEnviro Portal as a comprehensive web-based application for electronic permit applications, compliance reporting, emissions reporting, public notice tracking, and fee payment — consolidating multiple legacy systems into one platform. It is being expanded in phases to cover air quality, medical waste, and water programs.

action_tool_name: Establishing Clear Timeline Framework for Building Permits
reform_category: Promote Certainty in Permitting Process
description: HB493 established a 30-day deadline for local governments to complete building permit plan reviews. If the local government review exceeds the 30-day deadline, the applicant may hire an independent, qualified contractor to conduct the review instead, at a 50% reduction in regulatory fees.

action_tool_name: Eliminating Permitting Oversight Bodies
reform_category: Change Decision-Making Level
description:
Georgia Senate Bill 96 eliminated two advisory bodies which served as formal channels through which stakeholders could advise or intervene in permitting processes. 

- [The Environmental Advisory Council](https://law.justia.com/codes/georgia/title-12/chapter-2/article-1/section-12-2-2/) - Part of the Department of Natural Resources' Environmental Protection Division, the Council was a a 15-member advisory body which advised state environmental regulations and permitting policy. 
- [The Stakeholder Advisory Board](https://law.justia.com/codes/georgia/title-12/chapter-7/section-12-7-20/) - Was administered by the Georgia Soil and Water Conservation Commission (GSWCC) and jointly operated with the Environmental Protection Division. The board primarily created and maintained education and training programs for professionals working in the erosion and sedimentation field, however was also considered a channel for community feedback to permitting processes as members were community representatives or representatives from local governments/conservation districts.

In the void of any erosion and sedimentation oversight formerly provided by the Stakeholder Advisory Board, any relevant duties are consolidated under the [Erosion and Sediment Control Overview Council](https://law.justia.com/codes/georgia/title-12/chapter-7/section-12-7-20/), a 9-member group of state officials.

action_tool_name: Creating an intervenor support grant program
reform_category: Benefits for Nature/Communities
description: Provides underserved stakeholders up to $500,000 to intervene in DPU and EFSB proceedings.

action_tool_name: Extending the shelf-life of permits
reform_category: Promote Certainty in Permitting Process
description: A provisions of the 2024 Permit Extension Act allows for automatic extension of most state and local permits for land use projects by 2 years. It also revives permits that expired between January 1, 2023, and January 1, 2025. Similar versions of this legislation were passed in 2010 and 2012.

action_tool_name: Promoting transit-oriented housing development
reform_category: Promote Certainty in Permitting Process,Set Projects up for Success
description: WA Chapter 267, 2025 Laws (Promoting transit-oriented housing development) requires cities planning under the Growth Management Act (GMA) to allow multifamily housing developments that meet defined criteria in station areas near major transit stops. It also provides a 20 year tax exemption for qualifying projects.

action_tool_name: Establishing the Blueprint for Utah's Infrastructure and Development (BUILD) Coordinating Council
reform_category: Shift Culture,Set Projects up for Success,Utilize Data and Technology,Change Decision-Making Level
description:
Executive Order 2025-06 established the BUILD (Blueprint for Utah's Infrastructure, Land, and Development) Coordinating Council to align long-range planning across state agencies addressing housing, transportation, water, energy, open space, and air quality. The council builds on HCR 11 (2024), which encouraged agencies to account for cross-issue growth impacts in funding and policy decisions. 
This council is headquartered at the Utah State Capitol in Salt Lake City, and its members include Chair Laura Hanson (State Planning Coordinator) as well as senior representatives from twelve state agencies and civic organizations.
Overall, this represents the creation of a new interagency coordination body as a standing institutional mechanism.

action_tool_name: Allowing municipal governments to streamline residential permitting in Priority Housing Zones
reform_category: Consolidate or Eliminate Steps,Eliminate Permits/Permitting
description:
A 2025 CT Law establish a voluntary "priority housing development zone" program, to be administered by the Department of Housing. To qualify, such zones must meet three criteria: they must be situated within an existing residential or commercial district that is appropriate for the kinds of development the program contemplates; they must be consistent with the state plan of conservation and development; and they must cover at least ten percent of the municipality's developable land

**The zone's regulations must satisfy several conditions. **

They must align with the municipal or regional housing growth plan and be reasonably expected to boost the production of new dwelling units — notably, affordability is not a stated requirement. They must also fulfill the obligations of General Statutes §§ 8-2(b)(4-6) by providing housing opportunities, including multi-family options, for all residents of both the municipality and the region; promoting housing choice and economic diversity; and explicitly permitting the types of housing identified in the state's consolidated housing plan and the State Plan of Conservation and Development.

Beyond those baseline requirements, the regulations must allow multi-family housing by right, and must set minimum density thresholds of four units per acre for single-family homes, six per acre for townhouses and duplexes, and ten per acre for multi-family buildings. Finally, project approvals may require only site plan or subdivision review — special permits and special exceptions are not permitted as prerequisites.

action_tool_name: Improving permitting in housing and across sectors
reform_category: Shift Culture,Consolidate or Eliminate Steps,Promote Certainty in Permitting Process
description:
- Each principal executive-branch department that issues housing-related permits must designate a senior point of contact to coordinate and speed up processing.
- Those departments must submit updated permit-processing procedures and timelines to the Governor and the Maryland Coordinated Permitting Review Council by January 1, 2026.
- The order requires standards for third-party reviewers who can help expedite permit reviews at the applicant’s expense, including qualifications, conflict rules, and procedures for accepting or rejecting their recommendations.
- Where multiple state agencies or levels of government must review a permit, the order says reviews should be simultaneous rather than sequential, to the extent allowed by law.
- Agencies must recommend ways to increase predictability and transparency in housing-related permit applications, fully digitize permit applications and permit fee payments within budget authority, and seek to reduce processing times whenever possible.
EO_Leg_ID: EO 01.01.2025.19 / ADDRESSING MARYLAND'S AFFORDABLE HOUSING CRISIS

action_tool_name: Updating Renewable Energy & Storage Siting Process
reform_category: Change Decision-Making Level,Benefits for Nature/Communities,Set Projects up for Success,Promote Certainty in Permitting Process
description:
Shift Decision-making level

Public Act 233 of 2023 transferred authority over siting of large renewable energy facilities (100 MW+) from local governments to the Michigan Public Service Commission (MPSC) at the state level, preempting local zoning for those projects. Local governments can effectively no longer deny the approval of any new projects - although there are pathways for governments to adopt state approved sitting ordinances that all them to retain local control over siting.

Ensure Meaningful Benefits for Nature and Communities

Public Act 233 of 2023 requires "host community agreements" (HCAs) for large renewable energy facilities sited under Michigan Public Service Commission (MPSC) authority. These are legally required agreements between developers and host communities. If an affected local government refuses to enter into a host community agreement after good-faith negotiations with the applicant, the applicant may enter into a community benefits agreement with 1 or more community-based organizations within, or that serve residents of, the affected local unit. The amount paid by the applicant under this subsection must be equal to, or greater than, what the applicant would pay to the affected local government. Community benefits agreements must prioritize benefits to the community in which the energy facility is to be located. 

Public Act 233 of 2023 also requires a minimum payment of $2,000 per MW of capacity per year to host communities for large renewable energy projects.

Set Projects up for Success

Developers of large renewable energy projects are required by PA 233 to conduct pre-application consultations with state agencies (EGLE, DNR, SHPO, MDARD) and tribal governments before filing a siting application. MPSC staff are also available for pre-application meetings via a formal questionnaire process. 

Promoting Certainty

The 2023 law also created a "contested case" process with defined procedural steps and timelines at the MPSC for utility-scale renewable energy siting applications, giving developers a more predictable timeline and process compared to fragmented local approvals.

action_tool_name: Creating a Permitting Fast-track Program for Priority Housing Projects
reform_category: Consolidate or Eliminate Steps,Shift Culture
description:
The Executive Order hopes to positively impact housing affordability by establishing a "Housing Fast Lane" which will prioritize housing/infrastructure projects that meet certain standards to reduce permitting timelines to a target of 120 business days. The Housing Fast Lane will put a project in simultaneous multi-agency review, a single point of contact, and reduced traffic requirements to remove timely barriers to housing projects. Furthermore, the Order "Residential Development" permit pathway for Priority Housing Projects for relevant DNREC permits.

The Order also establishes state standards for priority projects which further state housing goals, streamlining priority commercial mixed-use, broadband, and water and sewer projects through the fast-track program.  Such projects meeting certain criteria are eligible for selection for the fast-track program. 

Furthermore, for projects which require environmental review, the Order directed DNREC to create a "Residential Development" permit pathway to streamline environmental review for Priority Housing Projects.
relevant_urls:
- [Text of EO ](https://news.delaware.gov/2026/02/26/governor-matt-meyer-signs-executive-order-streamlining-state-permitting-regulations/#:~:text=can%20be%20found-,here,-%2C%C2%A0as%20well%20as)
- [Governor's Office Press Release](https://news.delaware.gov/2026/02/26/governor-matt-meyer-signs-executive-order-streamlining-state-permitting-regulations/)
- [EO FAQ ](https://www.dropbox.com/scl/fi/e8rde5jivy7g1k2ph0ndf/Permitting-Accelerator-FAQ.pdf?rlkey=y09rctvcyo6oxxf4w6pxkac3u&st=q9yo9444&dl=0)
- [EO Implementation Guidelines](https://governor.delaware.gov/wp-content/uploads/sites/219/2026/02/1043_001.pdf)
EO_Leg_ID: Executive Order #18 - Implementing the Delaware Permitting Accelerator

action_tool_name: Establishing a Mandatory Statewide Two-step Administrative Subdivision Review Process with Statutory Timelines
reform_category: Consolidate or Eliminate Steps,Promote Certainty in Permitting Process,Set Projects up for Success,Change Decision-Making Level
description:
Before Chapter 501, 2023 subdivision review in Utah was governed by variable local processes with statewide timeline requirements. The bill standardized that process by statute, mandating a two-step administrative review, requiring designation of a dedicated review authority separate from the planning commission, and imposing a 40-day maximum review timeline for residential plats. 
This converts what had been an open-ended local discretionary process into an administrative one, with state code governing as a default if local ordinances were not updated by the compliance deadline.

action_tool_name: Eliminating Discretionary Review for Accessory Dwelling Units
reform_category: Consolidate or Eliminate Steps,Promote Certainty in Permitting Process,Change Decision-Making Level,Set Projects up for Success
description:
Senate Bill 1354 supersedes HB 166 (2023) as a comprehensive accessory dwelling unit reform, covering both attached and detached units. 
This legislation limits both local zoning authority and homeowners associations covenant authority, removing public law and private law blocking mechanisms at the same time. The prohibition on parking requirements (with limited exceptions) and setback parity removes conditions that had made accessory dwelling units economically impractical to build.
This bill also defined size protections, parking standards and setback parity rules among others in order to give applicants statewide objective criteria.

action_tool_name: Cataloging of State Permits
reform_category: Consolidate or Eliminate Steps,Eliminate Permits/Permitting,Change Decision-Making Level,Shift Culture
description: Executive Directive 2023-04 (August 2023) required all state agencies to catalog their permits and identify obsolete, duplicative, or unnecessary requirements for elimination. The results of this cataloging exercise are not immediately clear.

action_tool_name: Preventing Excessive Conditions on the Approval of Facilities that Process Compost
reform_category: Shift Culture,Promote Certainty in Permitting Process
description: Florida Chapter 2026-7 provides that a local government permit or other approval for a facility that processes compost may not be conditioned on the purchase of additional property by the applicant to expand the footprint of an existing privately-owned road.

action_tool_name: Expanding Use of Mitigation Credits Outside of Service Area
reform_category: Benefits for Nature/Communities
description: Florida Chapter 2025-191 allows permit applicants a one-time use of credits from a mitigation bank outside the mitigation bank service area when an insufficient number or type of credits are available within the impacted area.

action_tool_name: Establishing a Fast Track for High-Impact Economic Development and Infrastructure Projects
reform_category: Shift Culture,Change Decision-Making Level,Consolidate or Eliminate Steps,Promote Certainty in Permitting Process,Set Projects up for Success
description: [PA Permit Fast Track Program](https://www.pa.gov/agencies/oto/fasttrack) is available to significantly large economic development projects. If a project is enrolled in the program, it can receive accelerated state permitting reviews.

action_tool_name: Accelerating and Prioritizing State Siting and Permitting for Wind and Solar Projects.
reform_category: Shift Culture,Consolidate or Eliminate Steps
description: The Oregon Department of Energy (ODOE) must actively identify and flag solar and wind projects seeking state siting approvals that face the July 4, 2026 deadline to begin construction and qualify for federal clean energy tax credits. This identification requirement ensures the agency can pinpoint which developers need expedited processing to avoid losing up to 30% of project costs in federal incentives if they miss the deadline. Projects demonstrating secured utility contracts and benefits to Oregon ratepayers receive highest priority under this identification framework.

action_tool_name: Creating Voluntary Affordable Homeownership Density Bonus for Single-Family Residential Units
reform_category: Promote Certainty in Permitting Process,Change Decision-Making Level,Set Projects up for Success
description:
This law is an incentive-based tool that creates a new pathway for single-family residential projects by making higher density a local option contingent on affordability conditions. 
This law establishes a tool that cities can use voluntarily, wherein cities that adopt the density bonus framework provide developers a path to denser projects. 
Lastly, there are now clear criteria (60% owner-occupancy deed-restriction, 25% affordable units, 25% under 1600 sq ft) that trigger the density bonus entitlement.

action_tool_name: Establishing Infrastructure Office to Oversee Permitting for Large Projects
reform_category: Promote Certainty in Permitting Process,Change Decision-Making Level,Shift Culture,Set Projects up for Success
action_tool_type: Reorganization or Creation of New Government Offices
description:
Created under ED 2022-6 (2022) and further strengthened by ED 2023-04, the MIO serves as a single point of contact for applicants on large infrastructure projects ($50M+), coordinating permitting across all relevant state agencies. It requires agencies to develop coordinated permitting timelines and mandates a public dashboard to track permit progress.  The MIO acts as a single point of accountability across agencies to keep projects on schedule. 

The Office also coordinates with the Governor's cabinet on broader policy-making for infrastructure financing and approvals

action_tool_name: Streamlining the Administrative Process for Lot Divisions
reform_category: Shift Culture,Consolidate or Eliminate Steps
description: Washington's 2025 legislative session advanced several measures to streamline local land use permitting for housing. One law required cities to establish an administrative process for splitting a single lot into two, with criteria set by the state and no pre-decision public hearing required. A second law created a new "unit lot subdivision" process for urban growth areas, mandating clear objective standards, notice to nearby property owners, and specific plat disclosures, with implementation tied to the next comprehensive plan update or within two years. A third law expanded binding site plans—previously limited to commercial and industrial properties—to cover residential and mixed-use development as well. Taken together, the three laws push local governments toward faster, more ministerial review processes and away from discretionary public hearings for a broader range of housing and development decisions.

action_tool_name: Preventing new administrative rules
reform_category: Consolidate or Eliminate Steps,Promote Certainty in Permitting Process
description: Governor Kay Ivey signed an executive order on March 8, 2023 establishing a moratorium on new administrative rules through March 1, 2024

action_tool_name: Reducing Timelines to Approval for Development Projects
reform_category: Consolidate or Eliminate Steps
description:
HB 104 and HB 102 were both introduced and passed as a parts of the "Ready in 6" bill package. 

 HB 102 amends Title 17 of the Delaware Code to expedite commercial and economic development projects by creating strict deadlines for the issuance of entrance permits, whereas before there was no statutory deadline for this process. The Act establishes a 10 business day deadline to issue a temporary entrance permit after receiving complete applications and materials, so that construction can continue even as DelDOT completes further review. 

HB 104 amends Title 29 of the Delaware Code to create a categorical exclusion from the Preliminary Land Use Service (PLUS) pre-application review process, which is a multi-agency review process that can take several months. Economic development projects that meet certain standards are exempted from this process. 

Together, these Acts eliminate bottlenecks at the front and back ends of the permitting process that slow approval.

action_tool_name: Providing Zoning Reform Technical Assistance to Local Governments
reform_category: Consolidate or Eliminate Steps
description:
Senate Joint Resolution 8 directs the DSHA to provide free technical assistance to local governments to reform zoning codes in efforts to eliminate time-consuming barriers or unnecessary permit denials. 

Participation in this program to modernize zoning codes is voluntary, with participants needing to apply to receive free assistance. Participants select from several options the program offers and works with DSHA towards implementation, attending workshops and meetings offered by the department.

action_tool_name: Simplifying and aligning programs and policies
reform_category: Shift Culture,Consolidate or Eliminate Steps,Set Projects up for Success,Promote Certainty in Permitting Process
description:
- Simplified the State’s Designation Program to encourage new development and redevelopment in compact designated areas.
- Aligned policies and added technical assistance for communities.
- Charged the Board with developing rules and reviewing regional plans and maps to implement the tiered framework.

action_tool_name: Investing in Permitting Workforce to Expedite Timelines
reform_category: Building the Permitting Workforce,Promote Certainty in Permitting Process
description:
Governor McMaster announced he had appropriated funds of $13.7 million to invest in the necessary permitting workforce to tackle the backlog of environmental permit applications, and as a condition for this appropriation, he set a requirement for SCDES to make permitting decisions within a 90-day window. 

Gov. McMaster: "_My Executive Budget provides the DES with an appropriation of $13.7 million to hire and train the additional personnel necessary to reduce the current backlog of existing environmental permit applications. In addition, with this appropriation, the agency will be required to issue future permitting decisions within 90 days of the application submission."_

action_tool_name: Providing Pre-Application Meetings for Water Permits
reform_category: Set Projects up for Success
description: ADEQ’s drinking water engineering review guidance explicitly offers optional pre‑application meetings for applicants planning to construct drinking water facilities, where applicants can bring relevant documents and discuss requirements with engineers before submitting. This helps clarify expectations, potentially preventing delays and misunderstandings in the permitting process.

action_tool_name: Restricting Lot Sizes Greater than 1,500 Square Feet in Cities of over 10,000 People
reform_category: Change Decision-Making Level,Consolidate or Eliminate Steps,Set Projects up for Success,Promote Certainty in Permitting Process
description:
This bill removes the ability for Idaho's cities to impose any minimum lot size they chose for property development. This bill removes that tool for cities with a population above 10,000 in qualifying subdivisions of four acres or more, mandating lot sizes down to 1,500 square feet and densities of up to 12 units per acre. 
The infrastructure capacity exception preserves a local veto for infrastructure constraints. 
Overall, this bill establishes statewide minimum standards for subdivision approval.

action_tool_name: Prohibiting Excessive Requirements for Accessory Dwelling Units
reform_category: Shift Culture
description: West Virginia Chapter 241, Acts, Regular Session, 2026 prohibits municipalities from adopting policies that restrict accessory dwelling units on lots with  a single-family dwelling. This includes parking requirements, exterior design requirements, requirements to improve public streets and minimum building heights more restrictive than single-family dwellings.

action_tool_name: Resources for Michigan Wetland Mitigation
reform_category: Benefits for Nature/Communities
description:
Michigan EGLE has published guidance and requirements for wetland compensatory mitigation. The state requires compensatory mitigation for impacts to wetlands exceeding one-third acre. The requirements can be waived if the primary purpose of the work is to create or restore wetlands.

Acceptable mitigation methods include wetland restoration, creation, enhancement, or preservation, and can be achieved through mitigation banking, in-lieu fee programs, or permittee-responsible mitigation. EGLE provides guidance documents and an approved mitigation bank list.

action_tool_name: Streamlining of State Regulatory Framework
reform_category: Shift Culture,Consolidate or Eliminate Steps
description: West Virginia EO 6-25 aims to "reduce of outdated, overly complex, and inconsistent rules" by instructing all cabinet secretaries and department heads to identify confusing or inconsistent rules being enforced by their agencies. Agencies are instructed to make recommendations to the Governor of any strategies to simplify or streamline identified rules within 100 days of the order.

action_tool_name: Automatically granting intervenor status to host communities
reform_category: Benefits for Nature/Communities
description: To ensure communities have a voice in siting decisions, the 2024 Climate Act ensure municipalities that are hosting energy infrastructure facilities before the EFSB will be granted automatic intervenor status in EFSB proceedings.

action_tool_name: Establishing a Build Ready Program
reform_category: Consolidate or Eliminate Steps,Promote Certainty in Permitting Process,Set Projects up for Success
description: Authorizes NYSERDA to identify underutilized sites (brownfields, landfills, former industrial sites), acquire site control, and conduct permitting, design, engineering, and interconnection studies to prepare sites for renewable energy development. NYSERDA then transfers development rights to private developers through competitive RFP, with pre-completed environmental review and community engagement already in hand, expediting private-sector deployment on difficult-to-develop sites.

action_tool_name: Directs the Department of Environmental Quality and Division of Oil, Gas, and Mining to Assess and Recommend Expansions to Streamlined Permitting Pathways for Large Projects
reform_category: Utilize Data and Technology,Promote Certainty in Permitting Process,Consolidate or Eliminate Steps,Shift Culture
description: Executive Order 2025-01 directs the Utah Department of Environmental Quality and the Division of Oil, Gas, and Mining to identify opportunities to expand permitting by rule and general permitting for large, complex projects not currently eligible for those streamlined pathways, and to evaluate improvements to plantwide applicability limitation permit processes. Both agencies were required to engage stakeholders, benchmark other states' frameworks, and submit final reports to the Governor by October 1, 2025.

action_tool_name: Creating Clear and Standard Permitting Process for Wind Energy
reform_category: Change Decision-Making Level
description: Act 945 creates Arkansas's first-ever state permitting framework for wind energy facilities of 5 MW or larger exceeding 200 feet in height by granting the Arkansas Public Service Commission (APSC) exclusive authority to permit wind projects statewide.
